(**PNW**; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Nord-Ouest Pacifique*) is a geographic [region](/wiki/Region "Region") in [Western North America](/wiki/Western_North_America "Western North America") bounded by its coastal waters of the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") to the west and, loosely, by the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") to the east. Though no official boundary exists, the most common conception includes the [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), and [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho") and the Canadian province of [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"). Some broader conceptions reach north into [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") and [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon"), south into [Northern California](/wiki/Northern_California "Northern California"), and east into western [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"). Other conceptions may be limited to the coastal areas west of the [Cascade](/wiki/Cascade_Mountains "Cascade Mountains") and [Coast](/wiki/Coast_Mountains "Coast Mountains") mountains.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Region of northwestern North America

This article is about the region of northwestern North America and its Pacific Ocean coastal waters in Canada and the United States. For the U.S.-only region, see [Northwestern United States](/wiki/Northwestern_United_States "Northwestern United States"). For the wrestling territory, see [Pacific Northwest Wrestling](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest_Wrestling "Pacific Northwest Wrestling"). For the ocean, see [Northwest Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). For the phone company, see [Pacific Northwest Bell](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest_Bell "Pacific Northwest Bell").

The Pacific Northwest from [outer space](/wiki/Outer_space "Outer space")

The **Pacific Northwest** (**PNW**; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Nord-Ouest Pacifique*) is a geographic [region](/wiki/Region "Region") in [Western North America](/wiki/Western_North_America "Western North America") bounded by its coastal waters of the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") to the west and, loosely, by the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains") to the east. Though no official boundary exists, the most common conception includes the [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), and [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho") and the Canadian province of [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"). Some broader conceptions reach north into [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") and [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon"), south into [Northern California](/wiki/Northern_California "Northern California"), and east into western [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"). Other conceptions may be limited to the coastal areas west of the [Cascade](/wiki/Cascade_Mountains "Cascade Mountains") and [Coast](/wiki/Coast_Mountains "Coast Mountains") mountains.

The Northwest Coast is the coastal region of the Pacific Northwest, and the Northwest Plateau (also commonly known as "[the Interior](/wiki/British_Columbia_Interior "British Columbia Interior")" in British Columbia),[[1]](#cite_note-1) is the inland region. The term "Pacific Northwest" should not be confused with the [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territory "Northwest Territory") (also known as the Great Northwest, a historical term in the United States) or the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories") of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").

The region's largest metropolitan areas are [Greater Seattle](/wiki/Seattle_metropolitan_area "Seattle metropolitan area"), Washington, with 4 million people;[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Metro Vancouver](/wiki/Metro_Vancouver "Metro Vancouver"), British Columbia, with 3.4 million people;[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) [Greater Portland](/wiki/Portland_metropolitan_area "Portland metropolitan area"), Oregon, with 2.5 million people;[[4]](#cite_note-4) the [Boise, Idaho metropolitan area](/wiki/Boise,_Idaho_metropolitan_area "Boise, Idaho metropolitan area") with 845,877 people, and the [Spokane-Coeur d'Alene combined statistical area](/wiki/Spokane-Coeur_d%27Alene_combined_statistical_area "Spokane-Coeur d'Alene combined statistical area") with 793,285 people.

The culture of the Pacific Northwest is influenced by the [Canada–United States border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border"), which the United States and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") established at a time when the region's inhabitants were composed mostly of [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas"). Two sections of the border—one along the [49th parallel](/wiki/49th_parallel_north "49th parallel north") south of British Columbia and one between the [Alaska Panhandle](/wiki/Southeast_Alaska "Southeast Alaska") and northern British Columbia—have left a great impact on the region. According to Canadian historian [Ken Coates](/wiki/Ken_Coates_(historian) "Ken Coates (historian)"), the border has not merely influenced the Pacific Northwest—rather, "the region's history and character have been determined by the boundary".[[5]](#cite_note-coates-5)

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/35/PacNWComparison.PNG)](/wiki/File:PacNWComparison.PNG)

None of the multiple possible definitions of the Pacific Northwest is universally accepted. This map shows three possibilities: (1) The shaded area shows the historical [Oregon Country](/wiki/Oregon_Country "Oregon Country"). (2) The green line shows the [Cascadia bioregion](/wiki/Cascadia_(bioregion) "Cascadia (bioregion)").[[6]](#cite_note-sightline1-6) (3) The labeled states and provinces include Washington, Idaho, Oregon and British Columbia.

Definitions of the "Pacific Northwest" region vary, and even residents of the region do not agree on the exact boundary.[[7]](#cite_note-schwantes-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The most common conception includes the [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of Oregon, Washington, Idaho, and the [Canadian province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of British Columbia.[[5]](#cite_note-coates-5)

Broader definitions of the region have included the U.S. states of [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") and parts of the states of California, Montana, and Wyoming, and the Canadian territory of [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon").[[5]](#cite_note-coates-5)[[9]](#cite_note-Hayes1999-9)[[10]](#cite_note-sinclair-10)

Definitions based on the historic [Oregon Country](/wiki/Oregon_Country "Oregon Country") reach east to the [Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide_of_the_Americas "Continental Divide of the Americas"), thus including all of western [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") and western [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming"). Sometimes, the Pacific Northwest is defined as being the [Northwestern United States](/wiki/Northwestern_United_States "Northwestern United States") specifically, excluding [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Indigenous peoples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Indigenous peoples")]

See also: [Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Pacific_Northwest_Coast "Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast") and [Indigenous peoples of the Northwest Plateau](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Northwest_Plateau "Indigenous peoples of the Northwest Plateau")

The Pacific Northwest has been occupied by a diverse array of [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") for millennia. The Pacific Coast is seen by some scholars as a major [coastal migration route](/wiki/Coastal_migration_(Americas) "Coastal migration (Americas)") in the [settlement of the Americas](/wiki/Prehistoric_migration_and_settlement_of_the_Americas_from_Asia "Prehistoric migration and settlement of the Americas from Asia") by late Pleistocene peoples moving from northeast Asia into the Americas.[[11]](#cite_note-surovell2003-11)

The coastal migration hypothesis has been bolstered by findings such as the report that the sediments in the Port Eliza Cave[[12]](#cite_note-sfu2005-12) on [Vancouver Island](/wiki/Vancouver_Island "Vancouver Island") indicate the possibility of survivable climate as far back as 16 [kya](/wiki/Y_and_yr "Y and yr") (16,000 years ago) in the area, while the continental ice sheets were nearing their maximum extent.[[13]](#cite_note-alsuwaidi2006-13) Other evidence for human occupation dating back as much as 14.5 kya (14,500 years ago) is emerging from [Paisley Caves](/wiki/Paisley_Caves "Paisley Caves") in south-central Oregon as of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit).[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) However, despite such research, the coastal migration hypothesis is still subject to considerable debate.[[16]](#cite_note-jablonski2002-16)[[17]](#cite_note-turner2003-17)

Due in part to the richness of Pacific Northwest Coast and river fisheries, some of the indigenous peoples developed complex [sedentary](/wiki/Sedentism "Sedentism") societies, while remaining [hunter-gatherers](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer").[[18]](#cite_note-Diamond1999-18) The Pacific Northwest Coast is one of the few places where politically complex hunter-gatherers evolved and survived to historic contacts, and therefore has been vital for anthropologists and archaeologists seeking to understand how complex hunter and gatherer societies function.[[19]](#cite_note-Pauketat2012-19) When Europeans first arrived on the Northwest Coast, they found one of the world's most complex hunting and fishing societies, with large sedentary villages, large houses, systems of social rank and prestige, extensive trade networks, and many other factors more commonly associated with societies based on domesticated agriculture.[[19]](#cite_note-Pauketat2012-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Harris2001-20) In the interior of the Pacific Northwest, the indigenous peoples, at the time of European contact, had a diversity of cultures and societies. Some areas were home to mobile and egalitarian societies. Others, especially along major rivers such as the Columbia and Fraser, had very complex, affluent, sedentary societies rivaling those of the coast.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In British Columbia and Southeast Alaska, the [Haida](/wiki/Haida_people "Haida people") and [Tlingit](/wiki/Tlingit "Tlingit") erected large and elaborately carved [totem poles](/wiki/Totem_pole "Totem pole") that have become iconic of Pacific Northwest artistic traditions. Throughout the Pacific Northwest, thousands of indigenous people live, and some continue to practice their rich cultural traditions, "organizing their societies around cedar and salmon".[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Initial European exploration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Initial European exploration")]

Main article: [History of the west coast of North America](/wiki/History_of_the_west_coast_of_North_America "History of the west coast of North America")

Sailing for the Spanish Crown,
Portuguese navigator [Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo](/wiki/Juan_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Cabrillo "Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo") became in 1542 the first European to explore the west coast of today's United States and reached as far as Punta del Año Nuevo, north of [Monterrey](/wiki/Monterey,_California "Monterey, California"). [Bartolomé Ferrer](/wiki/Bartolom%C3%A9_Ferrer "Bartolomé Ferrer") continued on and may have reached as far north as the [Rogue River](/wiki/Rogue_River_(Oregon) "Rogue River (Oregon)") on the Oregon coast.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

By 1579, English captain and erstwhile [privateer](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer") [Francis Drake](/wiki/Francis_Drake "Francis Drake") sailed up the west coast of North America during the course of his [circumnavigation](/wiki/Francis_Drake%27s_circumnavigation "Francis Drake's circumnavigation") undertaking. Drake may have reached as far North as 48N and may have conducted some preliminary assessments of the western entry channels to the [Northwest Passage](/wiki/Northwest_Passage "Northwest Passage") under royal secrecy order. He then headed back south to land, to careen the ship, to rest and to continue on the undertaking. Drake and his crew eventually found a protected cove where they landed, in either the Pacific Northwest or Northern California. Contacts with a local indigenous population were established over the course of several weeks. While ashore, Drake claimed the area for Queen Elizabeth I as Nova Albion or [New Albion](/wiki/New_Albion "New Albion").[[25]](#cite_note-25) Various theories regarding the landing location of New Albion in the northern Pacific have been proposed, including the one recognized by the United States National Historic Landmark and California Historical Landmarks at [Point Reyes National Seashore](/wiki/Point_Reyes_National_Seashore "Point Reyes National Seashore").

By 1589, [Ortelius](/wiki/Abraham_Ortelius "Abraham Ortelius") was publishing the [Maris Pacifici](/wiki/Maris_Pacifici "Maris Pacifici") first ever Pacific map featuring on the west coast of North-America a first ever representation of two major coastline features: the mouth of the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River") identified as *"Rio Grande"* and the delta of the Fraser River labeled *"Baia de las isleas"*.

[Juan de Fuca](/wiki/Juan_de_Fuca "Juan de Fuca"), a [Greek](/wiki/Greece "Greece") captain sailing for the [Crown of Spain](/wiki/Crown_of_Spain "Crown of Spain"), supposedly found the [Strait of Juan de Fuca](/wiki/Strait_of_Juan_de_Fuca "Strait of Juan de Fuca") around 1592. The strait was named for him, but whether he discovered it or not has long been questioned.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

During the early 1740s, [Imperial Russia](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia") sent the Danish-born Russian [Vitus Bering](/wiki/Vitus_Bering "Vitus Bering") to the region.[[27]](#cite_note-27) By the late 18th century and into the mid-19th century, Russian settlers had established several posts and communities on the northwest Pacific coast, eventually reaching as far south as [Fort Ross, California](/wiki/Fort_Ross,_California "Fort Ross, California"). The [Russian River](/wiki/Russian_River_(California) "Russian River (California)") was named after this local presence and three ranch outposts located alongside.

In 1774, the viceroy of [New Spain](/wiki/New_Spain "New Spain") sent Spanish navigator [Juan Pérez](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_P%C3%A9rez_Hern%C3%A1ndez "Juan José Pérez Hernández") in the ship *Santiago* to the Pacific Northwest. Peréz made landfall on [Haida Gwaii](/wiki/Haida_Gwaii "Haida Gwaii") (Queen Charlotte Islands) on July 18, 1774. The northernmost latitude he reached was [54°40′ N](/wiki/Parallel_54%C2%B040%E2%80%B2_north "Parallel 54°40′ north").[[28]](#cite_note-ubcic.bc.ca-28) This was followed, in 1775, by another Spanish expedition, under the command of [Bruno de Heceta](/wiki/Bruno_de_Heceta "Bruno de Heceta") and including Juan Peréz and [Juan Francisco de la Bodega y Quadra](/wiki/Juan_Francisco_de_la_Bodega_y_Quadra "Juan Francisco de la Bodega y Quadra") as officers. On July 14, 1775, they landed on the [Olympic Peninsula](/wiki/Olympic_Peninsula "Olympic Peninsula") near the mouth of the [Quinault River](/wiki/Quinault_River "Quinault River"). On August 17, 1775, Heceta, returning south, sighted the mouth of the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River") and named it *Bahia de la Asunción*. While Heceta sailed south, Quadra continued north in the expedition's second ship, *Sonora*, reaching [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), at [59° N](/wiki/59th_parallel_north "59th parallel north").[[29]](#cite_note-hayes-29) In 1778 English mariner [Captain James Cook](/wiki/Captain_James_Cook "Captain James Cook") visited [Nootka Sound](/wiki/Nootka_Sound "Nootka Sound") on Vancouver Island and also voyaged as far as [Prince William Sound](/wiki/Prince_William_Sound "Prince William Sound").

In 1779, a third Spanish expedition, under the command of [Ignacio de Artega](/wiki/Ignacio_de_Arteaga_y_Baz%C3%A1n "Ignacio de Arteaga y Bazán") in the ship *Princesa*, and with Quadra as captain of the ship *Favorite*, sailed from Mexico to the coast of Alaska, reaching [61° N](/wiki/61st_parallel_north "61st parallel north"). Two further Spanish expeditions, in 1788 and 1789, both under [Esteban Jose Martínez](/wiki/Esteban_Jos%C3%A9_Mart%C3%ADnez_Fern%C3%A1ndez_y_Mart%C3%ADnez_de_la_Sierra "Esteban José Martínez Fernández y Martínez de la Sierra") and [Gonzalo López de Haro](/wiki/Gonzalo_L%C3%B3pez_de_Haro "Gonzalo López de Haro"), sailed to the Pacific Northwest. During the second expedition, they met the American captain [Robert Gray](/wiki/Robert_Gray_(sea-captain) "Robert Gray (sea-captain)") near [Nootka Sound](/wiki/Nootka_Sound "Nootka Sound"). Upon entering Nootka Sound, they found [William Douglas](/wiki/William_Douglas_(sea_captain) "William Douglas (sea captain)") and his ship *Iphigenia*. Conflict led to the [Nootka Crisis](/wiki/Nootka_Crisis "Nootka Crisis"), which was resolved by agreements known as the [Nootka Convention](/wiki/Nootka_Convention "Nootka Convention"). In 1790, the Spanish sent three ships to Nootka Sound, under the command of [Francisco de Eliza](/wiki/Francisco_de_Eliza "Francisco de Eliza"). After establishing a base at Nootka, Eliza sent out several exploration parties. [Salvador Fidalgo](/wiki/Salvador_Fidalgo "Salvador Fidalgo") was sent north to the Alaska coast. [Manuel Quimper](/wiki/Manuel_Quimper "Manuel Quimper"), with Gonzalo López de Haro as pilot, explored the Strait of Juan de Fuca, discovering the [San Juan Islands](/wiki/San_Juan_Islands "San Juan Islands") and [Admiralty Inlet](/wiki/Admiralty_Inlet "Admiralty Inlet") in the process. Francisco de Eliza himself took the ship *San Carlos* into the Strait of Juan de Fuca. From a base at [Port Discovery](/wiki/Port_Discovery,_Washington "Port Discovery, Washington"), his *pilotos* ([masters](/wiki/Master_(naval) "Master (naval)")) [José María Narváez](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Narv%C3%A1ez "José María Narváez") and [Juan Carrasco](/wiki/Juan_Carrasco_(explorer) "Juan Carrasco (explorer)") explored the San Juan Islands, [Haro Strait](/wiki/Haro_Strait "Haro Strait"), [Rosario Strait](/wiki/Rosario_Strait "Rosario Strait"), and [Bellingham Bay](/wiki/Bellingham_Bay "Bellingham Bay"). In the process, they discovered the [Strait of Georgia](/wiki/Strait_of_Georgia "Strait of Georgia") and explored it as far north as [Texada Island](/wiki/Texada_Island "Texada Island"). The expedition returned to Nootka Sound by August 1791. [Alessandro Malaspina](/wiki/Alessandro_Malaspina "Alessandro Malaspina"), sailing for Spain, explored and mapped the coast from [Yakutat Bay](/wiki/Yakutat_Bay "Yakutat Bay") to Prince William Sound in 1791, then sailed to Nootka Sound. Performing a scientific expedition in the manner of James Cook, Malaspina's scientists studied the [Tlingit](/wiki/Tlingit_people "Tlingit people") and [Nuu-chah-nulth](/wiki/Nuu-chah-nulth_people "Nuu-chah-nulth people") peoples before returning to Mexico. Another Spanish explorer, [Jacinto Caamaño](/wiki/Jacinto_Caama%C3%B1o "Jacinto Caamaño"), sailed the ship *Aranzazu* to Nootka Sound in May 1792. There he met Quadra, who was in command of the Spanish settlement and [Fort San Miguel](/wiki/Fort_San_Miguel "Fort San Miguel"). Quadra sent Caamaño north, to carefully explore the coast between Vancouver Island and [Bucareli Bay](/wiki/Bucareli_Bay "Bucareli Bay"), Alaska. Various Spanish maps, including Caamaño's, were given to George Vancouver in 1792, as the Spanish and British worked together to chart the complex coastline.[[29]](#cite_note-hayes-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/HMS_Discovery_1789_Vancouver.jpg/250px-HMS_Discovery_1789_Vancouver.jpg)](/wiki/File:HMS_Discovery_1789_Vancouver.jpg)

[HMS *Discovery*](/wiki/HMS_Discovery_(1789) "HMS Discovery (1789)") was the lead ship used by [George Vancouver](/wiki/George_Vancouver "George Vancouver").

From 1792 to 1794, [George Vancouver](/wiki/George_Vancouver "George Vancouver") charted the Pacific Northwest on behalf of Great Britain, including the Strait of Georgia, the bays and inlets of [Puget Sound](/wiki/Puget_Sound "Puget Sound"), and the [Johnstone Strait](/wiki/Johnstone_Strait "Johnstone Strait")–[Queen Charlotte Strait](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte_Strait "Queen Charlotte Strait") and much of the rest of the [British Columbia Coast](/wiki/British_Columbia_Coast "British Columbia Coast") and southeast Alaska shorelines.[[28]](#cite_note-ubcic.bc.ca-28) For him the city of [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver") and Vancouver Island are named, as well as [Vancouver, Washington](/wiki/Vancouver,_Washington "Vancouver, Washington"). From Mexico, Malaspina dispatched the last Spanish exploration expedition in the Pacific Northwest, under [Dionisio Alcalá Galiano](/wiki/Dionisio_Alcal%C3%A1_Galiano "Dionisio Alcalá Galiano") and [Cayentano Valdes](/wiki/Cayetano_Vald%C3%A9s_y_Flores "Cayetano Valdés y Flores") aboard the schooners *[Sutil](/wiki/Sutil_(ship) "Sutil (ship)")* and *[Mexicana](/wiki/Mexicana_(ship) "Mexicana (ship)")*.[[30]](#cite_note-30) They met Vancouver in the Strait of Georgia on June 21, 1792. Vancouver had explored Puget Sound just previously. The Spanish explorers knew of Admiralty Inlet and the unexplored region to the south, but they decided to sail north. They discovered and entered the [Fraser River](/wiki/Fraser_River "Fraser River") shortly before meeting Vancouver. After sharing maps and agreeing to cooperate, Galiano, Valdés, and Vancouver sailed north to [Desolation Sound](/wiki/Desolation_Sound "Desolation Sound") and the [Discovery Islands](/wiki/Discovery_Islands "Discovery Islands"), charting the coastline together. They passed through Johnstone Strait and [Cordero Channel](/wiki/Cordero_Channel "Cordero Channel") and returned to Nootka Sound. As a result, the Spanish explorers, who had set out from Nootka, became the first Europeans to circumnavigate Vancouver Island. Vancouver himself had entered the Strait of Juan de Fuca directly without going to Nootka first, so had not sailed completely around the island.[[29]](#cite_note-hayes-29)

In 1786, [Jean-François de La Pérouse](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Galaup,_comte_de_La_P%C3%A9rouse "Jean-François de Galaup, comte de La Pérouse"), representing France, sailed to Haida Gwaii after visiting Nootka Sound, but any possible French claims to this region were lost when La Pérouse and his men and journals were lost in a shipwreck near Australia. Upon encountering the Salish coastal tribes, either Pérouse or someone in his crew remarked, "What must astonish most is to see painting everywhere, everywhere sculpture, among a nation of hunters".[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Maritime fur trader](/wiki/Maritime_fur_trade "Maritime fur trade") [Charles William Barkley](/wiki/Charles_William_Barkley "Charles William Barkley") also visited the area in [*Imperial Eagle*](/wiki/Imperial_Eagle_(ship) "Imperial Eagle (ship)"), a British ship falsely flying the flag of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"). American merchant sea-captain [Robert Gray](/wiki/Robert_Gray_(sea-captain) "Robert Gray (sea-captain)") traded along the coast, and discovered the mouth of the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River").

### Continental crossover exploration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Continental crossover exploration")]

Explorer [Alexander Mackenzie](/wiki/Alexander_Mackenzie_(explorer) "Alexander Mackenzie (explorer)") completed in 1793 the first continental crossing in what is called today central [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") and reached the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). [Simon Fraser](/wiki/Simon_Fraser_(explorer) "Simon Fraser (explorer)") explored and mapped the [Fraser River](/wiki/Fraser_River "Fraser River") from Central British Columbia down to its mouth in 1808. And mapmaker [David Thompson](/wiki/David_Thompson_(explorer) "David Thompson (explorer)") explored in 1811 the entire route of the Columbia River from its northern headwaters all the way to its mouth. These explorations were commissioned by the [North West Company](/wiki/North_West_Company "North West Company") and were all undertaken with small teams of [Voyageurs](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs").

United States President [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") commissioned the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition") to travel through the Midwest starting from [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), cross the [Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide "Continental Divide") and reach the Columbia River up to its mouth. Americans reached the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") "overland" in 1805. The [Pacific Fur Company](/wiki/Pacific_Fur_Company "Pacific Fur Company") sent in 1811 an "over-lander" crew including a large contingent of [Voyageurs](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs") to retrace most of the path of the earlier expedition up to the mouth of the Columbia and join the company ship. The *[Tonquin](/wiki/Tonquin_(1807) "Tonquin (1807)")* came oversea via [Cape Horn](/wiki/Cape_Horn "Cape Horn") to build and operate [Fort Astoria](/wiki/Fort_Astoria "Fort Astoria").

These early land expeditions mapped the way for subsequent land explorations and building early settlements.

### Subsequent land explorations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Subsequent land explorations")]

Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(December 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The [Willamette River](/wiki/Willamette_River "Willamette River") was the first PNW inland waterway to be explored north–south during trapping expeditions carried out throughout the 1810s by the [Pacific Fur Company](/wiki/Pacific_Fur_Company "Pacific Fur Company") soon acquired by the [North West Company](/wiki/North_West_Company "North West Company") (NWC). During the 1820s, the upper Willamette, the [Umpqua](/wiki/Umpqua_River "Umpqua River"), the [Rogue](/wiki/Rogue_River_(Oregon) "Rogue River (Oregon)"), the [Klamath](/wiki/Klamath_River "Klamath River") were all reached still heading southward up toward the [Sacramento River](/wiki/Sacramento_River "Sacramento River") and [California](/wiki/California "California") under the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") (HBC) having now itself acquired the NWC. The [Siskiyou Trail](/wiki/Siskiyou_Trail "Siskiyou Trail") was gradually being established by [Alexander Roderick McLeod](/wiki/Alexander_Roderick_McLeod "Alexander Roderick McLeod") and [Peter Skene Ogden](/wiki/Peter_Skene_Ogden "Peter Skene Ogden") leading related expeditions for the HBC.

Also during the 1820s, HBC explorations were carried out northward originating from the [Columbia River](/wiki/Columbia_River "Columbia River") [Fort Astoria](/wiki/Fort_Astoria "Fort Astoria") long renamed to Fort George. Simon Plamondon first ventured during the early 20s into the [Cowlitz River](/wiki/Cowlitz_River "Cowlitz River") up to [Cowlitz Prairie](/wiki/Cowlitz_Prairie "Cowlitz Prairie"). By 1824, an expedition led by [James McMillan](/wiki/James_McMillan_(fur_trader) "James McMillan (fur trader)") was reaching [Puget Sound](/wiki/Puget_Sound "Puget Sound") via the [Chehalis River (Washington)](/wiki/Chehalis_River_(Washington) "Chehalis River (Washington)") and a portage. The same expedition went on all the way to [Boundary Bay](/wiki/Boundary_Bay "Boundary Bay") and reached the [Fraser River](/wiki/Fraser_River "Fraser River") via the [Nicomekl](/wiki/Nicomekl_River "Nicomekl River") and the [Salmon](/wiki/Salmon_River_(Langley) "Salmon River (Langley)") linked via a portage. The lower Fraser was revisited 16 years after explorer [Simon Fraser](/wiki/Simon_Fraser_(explorer) "Simon Fraser (explorer)") (NWC) had first reached its mouth, although originating from northern present-day [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"). Puget Sound soon after would get reached via the Cowlitz and the Cowlitz Landing portage, but originating from new HBC headquarter [Fort Vancouver](/wiki/Fort_Vancouver "Fort Vancouver") located closer by, north of the Columbia.

### Early settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early settlements")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/View_of_New_Archangel%2C_1837.tif/lossy-page1-250px-View_of_New_Archangel%2C_1837.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_New_Archangel,_1837.tif)

New Archangel (present-day [Sitka, Alaska](/wiki/Sitka,_Alaska "Sitka, Alaska")), the capital of Russian America

Noteworthy Russian settlements still in place include: [Unalaska](/wiki/Unalaska "Unalaska") (1774), [Kodiak](/wiki/Kodiak,_Alaska "Kodiak, Alaska") (1791), and [Sitka](/wiki/Sitka,_Alaska "Sitka, Alaska") (1804) making them the oldest permanent non-Indigenous settlements in the Pacific Northwest. Temporary Spanish settlement [Santa Cruz de Nuca](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Nuca "Santa Cruz de Nuca") (1789–1795) held on a few years at [Nootka Sound](/wiki/Nootka_Sound "Nootka Sound").

Other early occupation non-Indigenous settlements of interest, either long lasting or still in place, built and operated by either the [North West Company](/wiki/North_West_Company "North West Company"), the [Pacific Fur Company](/wiki/Pacific_Fur_Company "Pacific Fur Company") or the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") include: [Fort Saint-James](/wiki/Fort_St._James "Fort St. James") (1806; oldest in British Columbia west of the Rockies), [Fort Astoria](/wiki/Fort_Astoria "Fort Astoria") (1811; oldest in Oregon), [Fort Nez Percés](/wiki/Fort_Nez_Perc%C3%A9s "Fort Nez Percés") (1818), [Fort Alexandria](/wiki/Fort_Alexandria "Fort Alexandria") (1821), [Fort Vancouver](/wiki/Fort_Vancouver "Fort Vancouver") (1824), [Fort Langley](/wiki/Fort_Langley "Fort Langley") (1827; oldest in southern British Columbia), [Fort Nisqually](/wiki/Fort_Nisqually "Fort Nisqually") (1833), and [Fort Victoria](/wiki/Fort_Victoria_(British_Columbia) "Fort Victoria (British Columbia)") (1843).

Also of interest are the first mixed ancestry settlements sometimes referred as Métis settlements or French Canadian settlements. Native and newly arrived "half-breeds" (born out of "Europeans" and Indigenous alliances), local and newly arrived Indigenous people as well as "French Canadians" all issued of the fur trade were all able to peacefully coexist. Small scale farming occurred. Catholic missions and churches thrived for many years. These first settlements were: [French Prairie](/wiki/French_Prairie "French Prairie"), [Frenchtown](/wiki/Frenchtown,_Washington "Frenchtown, Washington") near Walla Walla, [Cowlitz Prairie](/wiki/Cowlitz_Prairie "Cowlitz Prairie") (Washington), [French Settlement](/wiki/French_Settlement,_Oregon "French Settlement, Oregon") (Oregon) and [Frenchtown](/wiki/Frenchtown,_Montana "Frenchtown, Montana") near Missoula. Most mixed ancestry people ended up resettled in or around Indigenous reserves during the subsequent period, or otherwise assimilating in the mainstream.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Boundary disputes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Boundary disputes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Pacific-Northwest.jpg/330px-Pacific-Northwest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pacific-Northwest.jpg)

U.S. Navy Lieutenant [Charles Wilkes](/wiki/Charles_Wilkes "Charles Wilkes")' 1841 Map of the [Oregon Territory](/wiki/Oregon_Territory "Oregon Territory") from "Narrative of the United States Exploring Expedition". Philadelphia: 1845

Initial formal claims to the region were asserted by Spain in 1513 with explorer [Nuñez de Balboa](/wiki/Vasco_N%C3%BA%C3%B1ez_de_Balboa "Vasco Núñez de Balboa"), the first European to sight the Pacific Ocean from the Americas. Russian [maritime fur trade](/wiki/Maritime_fur_trade "Maritime fur trade") activity, through the [Russian-American Company](/wiki/Russian-American_Company "Russian-American Company"), extended from the farther side of the Pacific to *[Russian America](/wiki/Russian_America "Russian America")*. This prompted Spain to send expeditions north to assert Spanish ownership, while Captain James Cook and subsequent expeditions by George Vancouver advanced British claims. As of the [Nootka Sound Conventions](/wiki/Nootka_Convention "Nootka Convention"), the last in 1794, Spain gave up its exclusive a priori claims and agreed to share the region with the other [powers](/wiki/Great_power "Great power"), giving up its garrison at Nootka Sound in the process.

The United States established a claim based on the discoveries of [Robert Gray](/wiki/Robert_Gray_(sea_captain) "Robert Gray (sea captain)"), the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition"), the construction of Fort Astoria, and the acquisition of Spanish claims given to the United States in the [Adams–Onís Treaty](/wiki/Adams%E2%80%93On%C3%ADs_Treaty "Adams–Onís Treaty").[[33]](#cite_note-Miller2006-33) From the 1810s until the 1840s, modern-day Washington, Oregon, Idaho, and western Montana, along with most of British Columbia, were part of what the United States called the [Oregon Country](/wiki/Oregon_Country "Oregon Country") and Britain called the [Columbia District](/wiki/Columbia_District "Columbia District"). This region was jointly claimed by the United States and Great Britain after the [Treaty of 1818](/wiki/Treaty_of_1818 "Treaty of 1818"), which established a co-dominion of interests in the region in lieu of a settlement. In 1840, American [Charles Wilkes](/wiki/Charles_Wilkes "Charles Wilkes") explored in the area. [John McLoughlin](/wiki/John_McLoughlin "John McLoughlin"), Chief Factor of the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company"), headquartered at Fort Vancouver, was the *de facto* local political authority for most of this time.

This arrangement ended as U.S. settlement grew and President [James K. Polk](/wiki/James_K._Polk "James K. Polk") was elected on a platform of calling for annexation of the entire Oregon Country and of Texas. After his election, supporters coined the famous slogan "Fifty-four Forty or Fight", referring to [54°40′ north latitude](/wiki/Parallel_54%C2%B040%E2%80%B2_north "Parallel 54°40′ north")—the northward limit of the United States' claim.[[34]](#cite_note-34) After a war scare with the United Kingdom, the [Oregon boundary dispute](/wiki/Oregon_boundary_dispute "Oregon boundary dispute") was settled in the 1846 [Oregon Treaty](/wiki/Oregon_Treaty "Oregon Treaty"), partitioning the region along the [49th parallel](/wiki/49th_parallel_north "49th parallel north") and resolving most, but not all, of the border disputes (see [Pig War](/wiki/Pig_War_(1859) "Pig War (1859)")).

The mainland territory north of the 49th parallel remained unincorporated until 1858, when a mass influx of Americans and others during the [Fraser Canyon Gold Rush](/wiki/Fraser_Canyon_Gold_Rush "Fraser Canyon Gold Rush") forced the hand of [Colony of Vancouver Island](/wiki/Colony_of_Vancouver_Island "Colony of Vancouver Island")'s Governor [James Douglas](/wiki/James_Douglas_(governor) "James Douglas (governor)"), who declared the mainland a [Crown colony](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony"). The two colonies were amalgamated in 1866 to cut costs, and joined the [Dominion of Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") in 1871. The U.S. portion became the [Oregon Territory](/wiki/Oregon_Territory "Oregon Territory") in 1848. It was later subdivided into Oregon Territory and [Washington Territory](/wiki/Washington_Territory "Washington Territory"). These territories became the states of Oregon, Idaho, Washington and parts of other Western states.

During the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), British Columbia officials pushed for London to invade and conquer the Washington Territory in effort to take advantage of Americans being distracted in the war on the Eastern region. This was rejected, as the UK did not wish to risk war with the United States, whose forces were better prepared and trained much more than the British troops.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

American expansionist pressure on British Columbia persisted after the colony became a province of Canada, even though Americans living in the province did not harbor [annexationist](/wiki/Annexationist_movements_of_Canada "Annexationist movements of Canada") inclinations. The [Fenian Brotherhood](/wiki/Fenian_Brotherhood "Fenian Brotherhood") openly organized and drilled in Washington, particularly in the 1870s and the 1880s, though no cross-border attacks were experienced. During the [Alaska Boundary Dispute](/wiki/Alaska_Boundary_Dispute "Alaska Boundary Dispute"), U.S. President [Teddy Roosevelt](/wiki/Teddy_Roosevelt "Teddy Roosevelt") threatened to invade and annex British Columbia if Britain would not yield on the question of the [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon") ports. In more recent times, during the so-called "[Salmon War](/wiki/Pacific_Salmon_War "Pacific Salmon War")" of the 1990s, Washington Senator [Slade Gorton](/wiki/Slade_Gorton "Slade Gorton") called for the [U.S. Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") to "force" the [Inside Passage](/wiki/Inside_Passage "Inside Passage"), even though it is not an official international waterway. Disputes between British Columbia and Alaska over the [Dixon Entrance](/wiki/Dixon_Entrance "Dixon Entrance") of the [Hecate Strait](/wiki/Hecate_Strait "Hecate Strait") between [Prince Rupert](/wiki/Prince_Rupert,_British_Columbia "Prince Rupert, British Columbia") and [Haida Gwaii](/wiki/Haida_Gwaii "Haida Gwaii") have not been resolved.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geology")]

Further information: [Geology of the Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Geology_of_the_Pacific_Northwest "Geology of the Pacific Northwest")

The Northwest is still highly geologically active, with both active [volcanoes](/wiki/Volcano "Volcano") and [geologic faults](/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

The last known great earthquake in the northwest was the [1700 Cascadia earthquake](/wiki/1700_Cascadia_earthquake "1700 Cascadia earthquake").[[38]](#cite_note-38) The geological record reveals that "great earthquakes" (those with moment magnitude 8 or higher) occur in the [Cascadia subduction zone](/wiki/Cascadia_subduction_zone "Cascadia subduction zone") about every 500 years on average, often accompanied by [tsunamis](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami"). There is evidence of at least 13 events at intervals from about 300 to 900 years.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Active [volcanoes in the region](/wiki/Cascade_Volcanoes "Cascade Volcanoes") include [Mount Garibaldi](/wiki/Mount_Garibaldi "Mount Garibaldi"), [Mount Baker](/wiki/Mount_Baker "Mount Baker"), [Mount Rainier](/wiki/Mount_Rainier "Mount Rainier"), [Mount St. Helens](/wiki/Mount_St._Helens "Mount St. Helens"), [Mount Adams](/wiki/Mount_Adams_(Washington) "Mount Adams (Washington)"), [Mount Hood](/wiki/Mount_Hood "Mount Hood"), [Mount Meager](/wiki/Mount_Meager_massif "Mount Meager massif"), [Mount Jefferson](/wiki/Mount_Jefferson_(Oregon) "Mount Jefferson (Oregon)"), [Mount Shasta](/wiki/Mount_Shasta "Mount Shasta"), [Lassen Peak](/wiki/Lassen_Peak "Lassen Peak") and [Glacier Peak](/wiki/Glacier_Peak "Glacier Peak").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Mount_Rainier_5917s.JPG/250px-Mount_Rainier_5917s.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mount_Rainier_5917s.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Mt._Hood_%288081466807%29.jpg/250px-Mt._Hood_%288081466807%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mt._Hood_(8081466807).jpg)

[Mount Rainier](/wiki/Mount_Rainier "Mount Rainier") (top) and [Mount Hood](/wiki/Mount_Hood "Mount Hood") (bottom) are the highest mountains in Washington and Oregon, and the 3rd and 20th [most prominent summits in the United States](/wiki/List_of_the_most_prominent_summits_of_the_United_States "List of the most prominent summits of the United States"), respectively.

The Pacific Northwest is a diverse geographic region, dominated by several mountain ranges, including the [Coast Mountains](/wiki/Coast_Mountains "Coast Mountains"), the [Cascade Range](/wiki/Cascade_Range "Cascade Range"), the [Olympic Mountains](/wiki/Olympic_Mountains "Olympic Mountains"), the [Columbia Mountains](/wiki/Columbia_Mountains "Columbia Mountains"), and the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains"). The highest peak in the Pacific Northwest is Mount Rainier, in the Washington Cascades, at 14,410 feet (4,392 m). Immediately inland from the Cascade Range are broad, generally dry plateaus. In the US, this region is known as the [Columbia Plateau](/wiki/Columbia_Plateau "Columbia Plateau"), while in British Columbia, it is the [Interior Plateau](/wiki/Interior_Plateau "Interior Plateau"), also called the [Fraser Plateau](/wiki/Fraser_Plateau "Fraser Plateau"). The Columbia Plateau was the scene of massive ice-age floods, and as a consequence, there are many [coulees](/wiki/Coulee "Coulee"), canyons, and the [Channeled Scablands](/wiki/Channeled_Scablands "Channeled Scablands"). Much of the plateau, especially in eastern Washington, is [irrigated](/wiki/Irrigated "Irrigated") [farmland](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land").[[40]](#cite_note-schillinger2010-40) The Columbia River cuts a deep and wide gorge around the rim of the Columbia Plateau and through the Cascade Range on its way to the Pacific Ocean.

Because many areas have plentiful rainfall and mild summers, the Pacific Northwest has some of North America's most lush and extensive forests, which are extensively populated with [Coast Douglas fir](/wiki/Pseudotsuga_menziesii_var._menziesii "Pseudotsuga menziesii var. menziesii") trees, the second tallest growing [evergreen](/wiki/Evergreen "Evergreen") [conifer](/wiki/Conifer "Conifer") on earth. The region also contains specimens of the [tallest trees](/wiki/List_of_tallest_trees "List of tallest trees") on earth, the [coast redwoods](/wiki/Sequoia_sempervirens "Sequoia sempervirens"), in southwestern Oregon, but the largest of these trees are located just south of the California border in northwestern California. Coastal forests in some areas are classified as [temperate rainforest](/wiki/Temperate_rainforest "Temperate rainforest").

Coastal features are defined by the interaction with the Pacific and the North American continent. The coastline of the Pacific Northwest is dotted by numerous fjords, bays, islands, and mountains. Some of these features include the [Oregon Coast](/wiki/Oregon_Coast "Oregon Coast"), [Burrard Inlet](/wiki/Burrard_Inlet "Burrard Inlet"), [Puget Sound](/wiki/Puget_Sound "Puget Sound"), and the highly complex fjords of the [British Columbia Coast](/wiki/British_Columbia_Coast "British Columbia Coast") and [Southeast Alaska](/wiki/Southeast_Alaska "Southeast Alaska"). The region has one of the world's longest [fjord](/wiki/Fjord "Fjord") coastlines.[[41]](#cite_note-Howe2010-41)

The Pacific Northwest contains an uncountable number of islands, many of the smaller ones being unnamed. The vast majority of such islands are in British Columbia and Alaska. Vancouver Island is by far the largest island in the area, but other significant land masses include the [Haida Gwaii](/wiki/Haida_Gwaii "Haida Gwaii"), vast and remote [Princess Royal Island](/wiki/Princess_Royal_Island "Princess Royal Island"), [Prince of Wales Island](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales_Island_(Alaska) "Prince of Wales Island (Alaska)") and [Chichagof Island](/wiki/Chichagof_Island "Chichagof Island"). The [Salish Sea](/wiki/Salish_Sea "Salish Sea") located close to major populated areas contains smaller but more frequently visited and well known islands. These include [Whidbey Island](/wiki/Whidbey_Island "Whidbey Island"), [Salt Spring Island](/wiki/Salt_Spring_Island "Salt Spring Island"), and [Texada Island](/wiki/Texada_Island "Texada Island"), along with dozens of smaller islands in the [San Juan](/wiki/San_Juan_Islands "San Juan Islands") and [Gulf Island](/wiki/Gulf_Islands "Gulf Islands") chains.

The major cities of Vancouver, [Portland](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), and [Tacoma](/wiki/Tacoma,_Washington "Tacoma, Washington") all began as [seaports](/wiki/Seaport "Seaport") supporting the logging, mining, and farming industries of the region, but have developed into major technological and industrial centers (such as the [Silicon Forest](/wiki/Silicon_Forest "Silicon Forest")), which benefit from their location on the [Pacific Rim](/wiki/Pacific_Rim "Pacific Rim").

If defined as British Columbia, Idaho, Oregon and Washington, the Pacific Northwest has four [US National Parks](/wiki/List_of_National_Parks_of_the_United_States "List of National Parks of the United States"): [Crater Lake](/wiki/Crater_Lake_National_Park "Crater Lake National Park") in Oregon, and [Olympic](/wiki/Olympic_National_Park "Olympic National Park"), [Mount Rainier](/wiki/Mount_Rainier_National_Park "Mount Rainier National Park"), and [North Cascades](/wiki/North_Cascades_National_Park "North Cascades National Park") in Washington. If a larger regional definition is used, then other US National Parks might be included, such as [Redwood National and State Parks](/wiki/Redwood_National_and_State_Parks "Redwood National and State Parks"), [Glacier Bay National Park](/wiki/Glacier_Bay_National_Park_and_Preserve "Glacier Bay National Park and Preserve"), [Wrangell–St. Elias National Park](/wiki/Wrangell%E2%80%93St._Elias_National_Park_and_Preserve "Wrangell–St. Elias National Park and Preserve"), [Grand Teton National Park](/wiki/Grand_Teton_National_Park "Grand Teton National Park"), and parts of [Glacier National Park](/wiki/Glacier_National_Park_(U.S.) "Glacier National Park (U.S.)") and [Yellowstone National Park](/wiki/Yellowstone_National_Park "Yellowstone National Park"). There are several [Canadian National Parks](/wiki/National_parks_of_Canada "National parks of Canada") in the Pacific Northwest, including [Pacific Rim National Park](/wiki/Pacific_Rim_National_Park "Pacific Rim National Park") on the west coast of Vancouver Island, [Mount Revelstoke National Park](/wiki/Mount_Revelstoke_National_Park "Mount Revelstoke National Park") and [Glacier National Park](/wiki/Glacier_National_Park_(Canada) "Glacier National Park (Canada)") in the Selkirk Range alongside [Rogers Pass](/wiki/Rogers_Pass_(British_Columbia) "Rogers Pass (British Columbia)"), [Kootenay National Park](/wiki/Kootenay_National_Park "Kootenay National Park") and [Yoho National Park](/wiki/Yoho_National_Park "Yoho National Park") on the British Columbia flank of the Rockies, [Gwaii Haanas National Park Reserve](/wiki/Gwaii_Haanas_National_Park_Reserve_and_Haida_Heritage_Site "Gwaii Haanas National Park Reserve and Haida Heritage Site") in Haida Gwaii, and the [Gulf Islands National Park Reserve](/wiki/Gulf_Islands_National_Park_Reserve "Gulf Islands National Park Reserve") in the Strait of Georgia. There are numerous [protected areas in British Columbia](/wiki/List_of_protected_areas_of_British_Columbia "List of protected areas of British Columbia") and [in the United States](/wiki/Protected_areas_of_the_United_States "Protected areas of the United States").

Other outstanding natural features include the [Columbia River Gorge](/wiki/Columbia_River_Gorge "Columbia River Gorge"), [Fraser Canyon](/wiki/Fraser_Canyon "Fraser Canyon"), [Mount St. Helens](/wiki/Mount_St._Helens "Mount St. Helens"), [Malaspina Glacier](/wiki/Malaspina_Glacier "Malaspina Glacier"), and [Hells Canyon](/wiki/Hells_Canyon "Hells Canyon"). The south-central Coast Mountains in British Columbia contain the five largest mid-latitude [icefields](/wiki/Icefield "Icefield") in the world.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Climate")]

The main general climatic types of the Pacific Northwest are temperate; cool temperatures and frequent cloudy skies are typical. Under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), a [warm-summer Mediterranean](/wiki/Warm-summer_Mediterranean "Warm-summer Mediterranean") (*Csb*) designation is assigned to many areas of the Pacific Northwest as far north as central Vancouver Island and the [Gulf Islands](/wiki/Gulf_Islands "Gulf Islands"), including cities such as [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_British_Columbia "Victoria, British Columbia"), Vancouver (coast area), Seattle, and Portland.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Other climate classification systems, such as [Trewartha](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification_scheme "Trewartha climate classification scheme"), place these areas in the oceanic zone (*Do*).[[43]](#cite_note-43) An [Alpine climate](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate") dominates in the high mountains. [Semi-arid](/wiki/Semi-arid "Semi-arid") and [arid](/wiki/Arid "Arid") climates are found east of the higher mountains, especially in [rainshadow](/wiki/Rainshadow "Rainshadow") areas. The [Harney Basin](/wiki/Harney_Basin "Harney Basin") of Oregon is an example of arid climate in the Pacific Northwest. [Humid continental](/wiki/Humid_continental "Humid continental") climates occur inland on windward sides, in places such as [Revelstoke, British Columbia](/wiki/Revelstoke,_British_Columbia "Revelstoke, British Columbia"). A [subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") can be found farther north, especially in Yukon and Alaska.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

The lack of rain in the hot season is associated with [high atmospheric pressure](/wiki/High-pressure_area "High-pressure area"). The shadows of the mountains also greatly decrease the amount of precipitation. West of the [Cascades](/wiki/Cascade_Range "Cascade Range"), the marine climates have a much greater precipitation than the west coast of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") due to [orographic lift](/wiki/Orographic_lift "Orographic lift"), with some regions seeing as much as 3,500 mm (138 in) of precipitation per year. Winters are very mild for the region's latitude. The growth of *[Arbutus](/wiki/Arbutus "Arbutus")*, an evergreen broad-leafed tree, is possible on Vancouver Island due to the mild winters.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)

#### The Big Dark

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: The Big Dark")]

**The Big Dark** is a term for winter in the Pacific Northwest. At a latitude of almost [48 degrees north](/wiki/48th_parallel_north "48th parallel north"), Seattle has sunsets before 6 PM between October and March, and fewer than nine hours of daylight for many weeks around the winter solstice.[[51]](#cite_note-Clarridge2019-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) The darkness contributes to [seasonal affective disorder](/wiki/Seasonal_affective_disorder "Seasonal affective disorder") among people living in northern cities, including those in the [Puget Sound region](/wiki/Puget_Sound_region "Puget Sound region").[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) The darkness is enhanced by a return from dry summers to extremely cloudy and wet weather characterized by recurring [atmospheric rivers](/wiki/Atmospheric_river "Atmospheric river") and [Pacific Northwest windstorms](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest_windstorm "Pacific Northwest windstorm").[[51]](#cite_note-Clarridge2019-51)[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### Ecoregions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ecoregions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Cascades_panorama_from_the_upper_meadow_below_Park_Butte.jpg/250px-Cascades_panorama_from_the_upper_meadow_below_Park_Butte.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cascades_panorama_from_the_upper_meadow_below_Park_Butte.jpg)

The Cascades range

Much of the Pacific Northwest is forested. The [Georgia Strait](/wiki/Georgia_Strait "Georgia Strait")–[Puget Sound](/wiki/Puget_Sound "Puget Sound") basin is shared between western British Columbia and Washington, and the [Pacific temperate rain forests](/wiki/Pacific_temperate_rain_forests "Pacific temperate rain forests") ecoregion, which is the largest of the world's [temperate rain forest](/wiki/Temperate_rain_forest "Temperate rain forest") [ecoregions](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") in the system created by the [World Wildlife Fund](/wiki/World_Wildlife_Fund "World Wildlife Fund"), stretches along the coast from Alaska to California. The dry desert inland from the [Cascade Range](/wiki/Cascade_Range "Cascade Range") and [Coast Mountains](/wiki/Coast_Mountains "Coast Mountains") is very different from the terrain and climate of the coastal area due to the [rain shadow effect](/wiki/Rain_shadow_effect "Rain shadow effect") of the mountains, and comprises the Columbia, Fraser and Thompson Plateaus and mountain ranges contained within them. The interior regions' climates largely within Eastern Washington, south central British Columbia, Eastern Oregon, and southern Idaho are a part of the [Great Basin Desert](/wiki/Great_Basin_Desert "Great Basin Desert"), although by their northern and eastern reaches, dry land and desert areas verge at the end of the Cascades' and Coast Mountains' [rain shadows](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow") with the boreal forest and various [alpine flora regimes](/wiki/Alpine_tundra "Alpine tundra") characteristic of eastern British Columbia, the [Idaho Panhandle](/wiki/Idaho_Panhandle "Idaho Panhandle") and western [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") roughly along a longitudinal line defined by the Idaho border with Washington and Oregon. The [North American inland temperate rainforest](/wiki/North_American_inland_temperate_rainforest "North American inland temperate rainforest") is in the so-called interior wet-belt, approximately 500–700 km inland from the Pacific coast on western, windward mountain slopes and valley bottoms of the [Columbia Mountains](/wiki/Columbia_Mountains "Columbia Mountains") and the [Rocky Mountains](/wiki/Rocky_Mountains "Rocky Mountains"). The interior wet-belt refers to a discontinuous band of humid forest patches, that are scattered over 1000 km between [Purden Lake](/wiki/Purden_Lake_Provincial_Park "Purden Lake Provincial Park") in Canada's [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") (54° North) and Montana and Idaho's [Bitterroot Mountains](/wiki/Bitterroot_Mountains "Bitterroot Mountains") and Idaho's [Salmon River Mountains](/wiki/Salmon_River_Mountains "Salmon River Mountains") (45° North).[[57]](#cite_note-57) It is closely associated with the [North Central Rockies forests](/wiki/North_Central_Rockies_forests "North Central Rockies forests") ecoregion designated by the WWF, which extends over a similar range but incorporates various non-temperate rainforest ecosystems.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Demographics")]

### Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Population")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/00/Cascadia_megacity_map.png/250px-Cascadia_megacity_map.png)](/wiki/File:Cascadia_megacity_map.png)

Map of most of the Cascadia urban megaregion, showing population density (shades of yellow/brown), highways (red), and major railways (black). Public land shown in shades of green. This map omits the southern Willamette Valley, which is typically considered part of the megaregion.

The overwhelming majority of the population of the Pacific Northwest is concentrated in an [urban megaregion](/wiki/Megaregions_of_the_United_States "Megaregions of the United States"), typically identified with its three major cities (Portland, Seattle, and Vancouver) but extending south from Vancouver to the [Eugene, Oregon](/wiki/Eugene,_Oregon "Eugene, Oregon") area, following the path of U.S. [Interstate 5](/wiki/Interstate_5 "Interstate 5"). Most of this urban megaregion is located between 40–90 miles inland from the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") coast.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

As of 2021, the combined populations of the [Lower Mainland](/wiki/Lower_Mainland "Lower Mainland") region (which includes the [Metro Vancouver Regional District](/wiki/Metro_Vancouver_Regional_District "Metro Vancouver Regional District")), the [Puget Sound region](/wiki/Puget_Sound_region "Puget Sound region") (which includes the [Seattle metropolitan area](/wiki/Seattle_metropolitan_area "Seattle metropolitan area")), and the [Willamette Valley](/wiki/Willamette_Valley "Willamette Valley") (which includes the [Portland metropolitan area](/wiki/Portland_metropolitan_area "Portland metropolitan area")) totaled more than ten million people.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) However, beyond these three urban areas, the PNW region is characterized by a very low density population distribution. Large geographical areas may only have one mid-sized to small-sized city as a regional center (often a county seat), with smaller cities and towns scattered around. Vast areas of the region may have little or no population at all, largely due to the presence of extensive mountains and forests, and plateaus containing both extensive farm and range lands, much of which is protected from development in large parks and preserves, or by zoning use regulation related to traditional land use. For example, all cities within the vast coastal area between Eugene, Oregon and [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), California, including [Southern Oregon](/wiki/Southern_Oregon "Southern Oregon") and [far northern California](/wiki/Upstate_California "Upstate California") (often included in the "Pacific Northwest") have populations less than 100,000, with that portion of the state containing millions of acres of national forests and parks.

The most densely populated region west of the [Cascade Range](/wiki/Cascade_Range "Cascade Range") outside this corridor is Southern [Vancouver Island](/wiki/Vancouver_Island "Vancouver Island") near British Columbia's Pacific coast, including [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_British_Columbia "Victoria, British Columbia") and [Nanaimo](/wiki/Nanaimo "Nanaimo"), (with a population of approximately 500,000).[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) In contrast, most of the coastal areas of Washington and [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon_Coast "Oregon Coast") are sparsely populated.

East of the Cascades, populated areas include the [Okanagan Valley](/wiki/Okanagan_Valley "Okanagan Valley") in the British Columbia interior centered around the city of [Kelowna](/wiki/Kelowna "Kelowna"), the [Yakima Valley](/wiki/Yakima "Yakima") and [Kennewick](/wiki/Kennewick "Kennewick") in Washington, the [Bend](/wiki/Bend,_Oregon "Bend, Oregon")-[Redmond](/wiki/Redmond,_Oregon "Redmond, Oregon") area just east of the Cascades in Central Oregon. Further east are urban areas including [Spokane](/wiki/Spokane,_Washington "Spokane, Washington") and [Boise](/wiki/Boise "Boise") that are sometimes included in the Pacific Northwest, but share relatively few ties with the core Vancouver-Seattle-Portland corridor.

### Ethnicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ethnicity")]

In British Columbia, [Europeans](/wiki/European_Canadians "European Canadians") form 64% of the population with [Asians](/wiki/Asians "Asians") comprising a further 29% of the provincial population. Both the Asian and European populations of the province are diverse; of the Asian population, 15% of the population is of [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asian_Canadians "East Asian Canadians") descent, 8% of the population is of [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asian_Canadians "South Asian Canadians") descent, with smaller numbers of Southeast Asians (4%) and [West Asians](/wiki/West_Asian_Canadians "West Asian Canadians") (2%); the European population contains large communities of [English Canadians](/wiki/English_Canadians "English Canadians"), [Scottish Canadians](/wiki/Scottish_Canadians "Scottish Canadians"), [Irish Canadians](/wiki/Irish_Canadians "Irish Canadians"), [French Canadians](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians"), [German Canadians](/wiki/German_Canadians "German Canadians"), and many others.[[81]](#cite_note-89) Europeans form between 80 and 90 per cent of the population in U.S. section of the Pacific Northwest, thus the Asian presence is comparably smaller, with all Asian groups together comprising about 8% of Washington state's population, and less than 4% in Oregon and Idaho. This is due to immigration quotas at the federal level, as while Canada has one-tenth the population of the United States, it takes in one-quarter as many immigrants, many of whom are from Asia. Vancouver settled about a quarter of all [emigrants from Hong Kong](/wiki/Emigration_from_Hong_Kong "Emigration from Hong Kong") to Canada in the late 1980s.[[82]](#cite_note-90)

In the U.S. side of the region, [Latinos](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") make up a large portion of the agricultural labor force east of the Cascade Range, and are an increasing presence in the general labor force west of the Cascades. Despite the [Second Great Migration](/wiki/Second_Great_Migration_(African_American) "Second Great Migration (African American)") from the 1940s to 1960s due to the booming Boeing war industry and post-war growing economy, [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") are less numerous in the Pacific Northwest; however, the overall African American population has been growing in other smaller urban areas throughout the region such as Eugene.[[83]](#cite_note-91) African Americans tend to concentrate in western urban areas such as Tacoma, south Seattle, and Portland. Nonetheless, Black people have a very large presence in Tacoma's [Hilltop](/wiki/Hilltop,_Tacoma,_Washington "Hilltop, Tacoma, Washington") and South Tacoma neighborhoods, Seattle's [Central District](/wiki/Central_District,_Seattle "Central District, Seattle") and [Rainier Valley](/wiki/Rainier_Valley,_Seattle "Rainier Valley, Seattle") neighborhoods,[[84]](#cite_note-92) and in Portland's Northeast Quadrant.[[85]](#cite_note-93) There are growing numbers in Vancouver as well, particularly Africans, Jamaicans and Black people from the United States.

Beginning in the late 20th century, a general [suburbanization](/wiki/Suburbanization "Suburbanization") of East and South Asian communities occurred in Vancouver, prompting concerns regarding the preservation of historical inner-city communities particularly in [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown "Chinatown") and [Punjabi Market](/wiki/Punjabi_Market "Punjabi Market").

African Americans have held the positions of Mayor in Seattle; King County executive, while the state of Washington elected a [Chinese American](/wiki/Chinese_American "Chinese American") governor during the 1990s, [Gary Locke](/wiki/Gary_Locke_(politician) "Gary Locke (politician)").

British Columbians of many ethnicities are prominent in all levels of politics and government, and the province has a number of "firsts" in Canadian political history, including the first non-white and Asian [Premier](/wiki/Premier_of_British_Columbia "Premier of British Columbia"), [Ujjal Dosanjh](/wiki/Ujjal_Dosanjh "Ujjal Dosanjh") (who is [Indo-Canadian](/wiki/Indo-Canadian "Indo-Canadian")) and the first Asian [Lieutenant-Governor](/wiki/Lieutenant-Governor_of_British_Columbia "Lieutenant-Governor of British Columbia"), the Hon. [David Lam](/wiki/David_Lam_(real_estate_entrepreneur) "David Lam (real estate entrepreneur)"). The Lieutenant-Governor from 2007 to 2012, [Steven Point](/wiki/Steven_Point "Steven Point"), was of aboriginal origin, being [Stó:lō](/wiki/St%C3%B3:l%C5%8D "Stó:lō") (the dominant type of [Coast Salish](/wiki/Coast_Salish_peoples "Coast Salish peoples") in BC's Lower Mainland) from the [Chilliwack](/wiki/Chilliwack "Chilliwack") area. The leader of the opposition party from 2005 to 2011, the [NDP](/wiki/British_Columbia_New_Democratic_Party "British Columbia New Democratic Party"), was [Carole James](/wiki/Carole_James "Carole James"), of partial [Métis](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_people_(Canada) "Métis people (Canada)") origin. Colonial governor [James Douglas](/wiki/James_Douglas_(governor) "James Douglas (governor)") was himself [mulatto](/wiki/Mulatto "Mulatto") of Guyanese extraction and his wife was of [Cree](/wiki/Cree "Cree") origin.

Oregon has been a national leader concerning [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") representation in government. At the time of his election to the office of Portland mayor in 2008, [Sam Adams](/wiki/Sam_Adams_(Oregon_politician) "Sam Adams (Oregon politician)") was the first openly gay individual to represent a city of Portland's size in the United States. In [Silverton, Oregon](/wiki/Silverton,_Oregon "Silverton, Oregon"), the same year, [Stu Rasmussen](/wiki/Stu_Rasmussen "Stu Rasmussen") was elected the first [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") mayor in U.S. history. The first two LGBT state supreme court justices in the country both sit on the [Oregon Supreme Court](/wiki/Oregon_Supreme_Court "Oregon Supreme Court"). At the time of her election, the previous [Governor of Oregon](/wiki/Governor_of_Oregon "Governor of Oregon") [Kate Brown](/wiki/Kate_Brown "Kate Brown") was the highest-ranking openly [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexual "Bisexual") politician in the United States. The current governor is [Tina Kotek](/wiki/Tina_Kotek "Tina Kotek"), who became one of the first two openly lesbian women elected governor of a U.S. state after her election in 2022. In 2017, [Jenny Durkan](/wiki/Jenny_Durkan "Jenny Durkan") was elected as the first openly lesbian mayor of Seattle.

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Language")]

Most Americans and Canadians consider the [Pacific Northwest English](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest_English "Pacific Northwest English") accent "neutral", though distinct from the Midwestern dialects that some believe typify American speech.[[86]](#cite_note-94)[[87]](#cite_note-95)[[88]](#cite_note-96) It possess the low back vowel merger, or the [cot–caught merger](/wiki/Cot%E2%80%93caught_merger "Cot–caught merger"). [Canadian raising](/wiki/Canadian_raising "Canadian raising") occurs in British Columbia and some speakers in Washington to a similar degree as it does in [southern Ontario](/wiki/Southern_Ontario "Southern Ontario"), but weaker than other parts of Canada. The [California Vowel Shift](/wiki/California_Vowel_Shift "California Vowel Shift") also affects speech in the region.

[Chinook Jargon](/wiki/Chinook_Jargon "Chinook Jargon") was a [pidgin](/wiki/Pidgin "Pidgin") or [trade language](/wiki/Trade_language "Trade language") established among [indigenous inhabitants of the region](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Pacific_Northwest_Coast "Indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast"). After contact with Europeans, French, English, and [Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language") words entered the language, and "eventually, Chinook became the lingua franca for as many as 250,000 people along the Pacific Slope from Alaska to Oregon".[[89]](#cite_note-97) Chinook Jargon reached its height of usage in the 19th century, though remained common in resource and wilderness areas, particularly, but not exclusively, by [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") and [Canadian First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada") people, well into the 20th century. Today, its influence is felt mostly in [place names](/wiki/Place_name "Place name") and a handful of localized [slang](/wiki/Slang "Slang") terms, particularly the word *[skookum](/wiki/Skookum "Skookum")*, which remains hallmark of people raised in the region.

French was the [voyageurs](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs")' working language of the early continental crossover exploration crews. The ensuing fur trade was dominated by French Canadian (and Métis) workers. The language held on South of the border in a few early settlements such as [French Prairie](/wiki/French_Prairie "French Prairie"), [Frenchtown (Washington)](/wiki/Frenchtown,_Washington "Frenchtown, Washington"), [Frenchtown (Montana)](/wiki/Frenchtown,_Montana "Frenchtown, Montana"), [Cowlitz Prairie](/wiki/Cowlitz_Prairie "Cowlitz Prairie"), and [French Settlement](/wiki/French_Settlement,_Oregon "French Settlement, Oregon"). These early settlements got resupplied through waves of new arrivals from the [Oregon Trail](/wiki/Oregon_Trail "Oregon Trail") attracted by the language and Catholics communities. Much of it ended up assimilating to the melting pot or sometimes folding into [reservations](/wiki/Indian_reservation "Indian reservation"). New waves of French speaking workers came in later on to work in forestry and wood mills such as [Maillardville](/wiki/Maillardville "Maillardville") in the [greater Vancouver](/wiki/Greater_Vancouver "Greater Vancouver") area. French remains much used in [place names](/wiki/Place_name "Place name"), in the documentation of products intended for North America (along with Spanish and English), as well as an official language in Canada. French schooling is also popular in [Western Canada](/wiki/Western_Canada "Western Canada"), including British Columbia.

Besides English and indigenous languages, [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") has been common since the [gold rushes](/wiki/Gold_rush "Gold rush") of the mid-19th century, most particularly in British Columbia. Since the 1980s, [Taishanese](/wiki/Taishanese "Taishanese"), a [Yue dialect](/wiki/Yue_dialect "Yue dialect") predominant in the area, has been replaced by mainstream [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") and by [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") because of large-scale immigration from Asia. [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") is also common in British Columbia, specifically in Greater Vancouver and the [Fraser Valley](/wiki/Fraser_Valley "Fraser Valley") owing to the large [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_people "Punjabi people") [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") population in the region, first arriving in the late 19th century.[[90]](#cite_note-WaltonRobertsThreeReadingsp316-98) [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") is also spoken in parts of Oregon and Washington as well as British Columbia by [Mexicans](/wiki/Mexicans "Mexicans") and other Hispanics, both recent immigrants and long-standing communities.
