The Pacific Northwest has the lowest rate of church attendance in the United States and consistently reports the highest percentage of [atheism](/wiki/Atheists "Atheists");[[95]](#cite_note-103)[[96]](#cite_note-104) this is most pronounced on the part of the region west of the Cascades.[[97]](#cite_note-105) A recent study indicates that one quarter of those in Washington and Oregon have no religion.[[98]](#cite_note-106) Similarly, according to the 2011 National Household Survey, 44% of British Columbia residents reported no religion.[[99]](#cite_note-107)

Religion plays a smaller part in Pacific Northwest politics than in the rest of the United States. The [religious right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right") has considerably less political influence than in other regions. Political conservatives in the Pacific Northwest tend to identify more strongly with free-market [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarianism "Libertarianism") values than they do with more religious [social conservatives](/wiki/Social_conservatives "Social conservatives").[[100]](#cite_note-108)

That said, three of the four major international [charities](/wiki/Charities "Charities") in the region are religious in nature: [World Concern](/wiki/World_Concern "World Concern"), [World Vision International](/wiki/World_Vision_International "World Vision International"), and [Mercy Corps](/wiki/Mercy_Corps "Mercy Corps"). This is part of a long tradition of activist religion. The [Skid Road](/wiki/Skid_Road "Skid Road") Group, a shelter offering soup and sermons to the [unemployed](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment") and recovering [alcoholics](/wiki/Alcoholics "Alcoholics"), was launched in Vancouver, with the [Salvation Army](/wiki/Salvation_Army "Salvation Army") having deep roots in the [Gastown](/wiki/Gastown "Gastown") district, dating back to the era of the construction of the [Canadian Pacific Railway](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Railway "Canadian Pacific Railway") (1880s) and attained prominence in the same centers during the [Klondike Gold Rush](/wiki/Klondike_Gold_Rush "Klondike Gold Rush").

The region is also known as a magnet for a wide range of philosophical and spiritual belief systems. Eastern spiritual beliefs have been adopted by an unusually large number of people (by North American standards), and [Tibetan Buddhism](/wiki/Tibetan_Buddhism "Tibetan Buddhism") in particular has a strong local following.[[101]](#cite_note-109) The Northwest Tibetan Cultural Association, claimed to be the largest organization of its kind in the world, was founded in Portland in 1993.

The region is home to many unique Christian communities, ranging from the [Doukhobors](/wiki/Doukhobors "Doukhobors") to the [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites"). The Mennonite Central Committee Supportive Care Services is based in the British Columbia city of Abbotsford.[[102]](#cite_note-110) The [Mennonite Central Committee](/wiki/Mennonite_Central_Committee "Mennonite Central Committee") and the [Mennonite Disaster Service](/wiki/Mennonite_Disaster_Service "Mennonite Disaster Service") enjoy a heavy rate of enlistment and donations from the strong Mennonite community in British Columbia's [Fraser Valley](/wiki/Fraser_Valley "Fraser Valley"). The Doukhobors, whose church is the Union of Spiritual Communities of Christ, are a Russian [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") sect whose migration to Canada was aided by Count [Leo Tolstoy](/wiki/Leo_Tolstoy "Leo Tolstoy"), and who are today focused in the [West Kootenay](/wiki/West_Kootenay "West Kootenay") and [Boundary](/wiki/Boundary_Country "Boundary Country") regions of Southeastern British Columbia. Their history in Canada includes resistance to state education and industrial development (see [Sons of Freedom](/wiki/Sons_of_Freedom_(political_group) "Sons of Freedom (political group)")). Also, within the region, there is a fairly strong representation of [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") churches (Greek, Russian, Serbian, and others), as well as the [Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church"). Oregon's [Willamette Valley](/wiki/Willamette_Valley "Willamette Valley") has a large population of [Russian Old Believers](/wiki/Old_Believers "Old Believers").[[103]](#cite_note-111) Religious sees that are based in the Pacific Northwest include the Roman Catholic [ecclesiastical provinces](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_province "Ecclesiastical province") of [Portland](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Portland_in_Oregon "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Portland in Oregon"), [Seattle](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Seattle "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Seattle"), and [Vancouver](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Vancouver "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Vancouver"), [Province 8 of the Episcopal Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Province_8_of_the_Episcopal_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Province 8 of the Episcopal Church in the United States of America"), the Anglican [Ecclesiastical Province of British Columbia and Yukon](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Province_of_British_Columbia_and_Yukon "Ecclesiastical Province of British Columbia and Yukon"), and the [suffragan](/wiki/Suffragan "Suffragan") dioceses that make up those provinces.

Yogic teachings, Sufism, tribal and ancient beliefs and other philosophies are widely studied and appreciated in the region. The [Lower Mainland](/wiki/Lower_Mainland "Lower Mainland") of British Columbia has a very large [Sikh](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") community. Oregon has a considerable [Quaker](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") ([Society of Friends](/wiki/Society_of_Friends "Society of Friends")) population. There has been major growth in [Chinese Buddhist](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism") temples since the increase in immigration from [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") in the 1980s, especially in Vancouver.

Also in Vancouver, there is a small [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") population, a number of Parsee ([Zoroastrians](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism")), and an emerging [Muslim](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), especially the 11,000-strong [Ismaili](/wiki/Ismaili "Ismaili"),[[104]](#cite_note-112) population from [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), the Middle East, Africa, the Balkans, Southeast Asia, and elsewhere.

Two of the five [Shingon](/wiki/Shingon "Shingon") Buddhist temples in America are in Seattle.[[105]](#cite_note-Lee_Matsuoka_Yee_Nakasone_2015_p._883-113)

Some people in the area also embrace alternative [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), such as [New Age](/wiki/New_Age "New Age") spirituality and [Neo-Paganism](/wiki/Neo-Paganism "Neo-Paganism").[[106]](#cite_note-114) A [New Thought](/wiki/New_Thought "New Thought") church called [Living Enrichment Center](/wiki/Living_Enrichment_Center "Living Enrichment Center") with 4,000 members was in [Wilsonville, Oregon](/wiki/Wilsonville,_Oregon "Wilsonville, Oregon"), from 1992 to 2004.[[107]](#cite_note-115)

* [Brother Twelve](/wiki/Brother_Twelve "Brother Twelve") ran a controversial commune in the [Gulf Islands](/wiki/Gulf_Islands "Gulf Islands") of British Columbia early in the 20th century.[[108]](#cite_note-116)
* The [Emissaries of Divine Light](/wiki/Emissaries_of_Divine_Light "Emissaries of Divine Light") are a notable presence in the region of [100 Mile House](/wiki/100_Mile_House "100 Mile House"), British Columbia and also have a large ashram on [Kootenay Lake](/wiki/Kootenay_Lake "Kootenay Lake"), northeast of [Nelson, British Columbia](/wiki/Nelson,_British_Columbia "Nelson, British Columbia").[[109]](#cite_note-117)
* The followers of the Guru [Rajneesh](/wiki/Rajneesh "Rajneesh"), the *[sannyasins](/wiki/Sannyasins "Sannyasins")*, established a center for their beliefs and lifestyle near [Antelope, Oregon](/wiki/Antelope,_Oregon "Antelope, Oregon"), which included an [ashram](/wiki/Ashram "Ashram") complex as well as, for a while, an attempted takeover of the local economy.[[110]](#cite_note-118)
* The training school of the immortal (according to the organization) being [Ramtha](/wiki/Ramtha "Ramtha") is headquartered in [Yelm, Washington](/wiki/Yelm,_Washington "Yelm, Washington").[[111]](#cite_note-119)
* [Eckhart Tolle](/wiki/Eckhart_Tolle "Eckhart Tolle"), author of *[The Power of Now](/wiki/The_Power_of_Now "The Power of Now")*, lives in Vancouver, British Columbia.[[112]](#cite_note-120)
* [Neale Donald Walsch](/wiki/Neale_Donald_Walsch "Neale Donald Walsch"), author of *[Conversations with God](/wiki/Conversations_with_God "Conversations with God")*, lives in Ashland, Oregon, where he runs a retreat center.[[113]](#cite_note-121)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Politics")]

A major divide in political opinion separates the region's greatly more populated urban core and rural areas west of the mountains from its less populated rural areas to their east and (in British Columbia and Alaska) north.[[114]](#cite_note-122) The coastal areas—especially in the cities of Vancouver, Victoria, Bellingham, Seattle, Tacoma, Olympia, Portland, and Eugene—are some of the most politically liberal parts of North America, regularly supporting left-wing political candidates and causes by significant majorities. The religious right has much less influence throughout the region than elsewhere in the U.S. or in Western Canada. Certain areas of the British Columbia Interior, particularly the [West Kootenay](/wiki/West_Kootenay "West Kootenay"), and some areas of Vancouver Island and the B.C. Coast, have long histories of [labour](/wiki/History_of_British_Columbia#Rise_of_the_labour_movement "History of British Columbia"), environmental, and social activism.

The jurisdictions have relatively liberal [abortion](/wiki/Abortion_rights "Abortion rights") laws, gender equality laws, legal [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)"), and strong [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights "LGBT rights"), especially British Columbia where these issues are of federal jurisdiction, and where same-sex marriage has been legal since 2003, Washington, where it has been legal since 2012, and Oregon, where same-sex marriage was made legal in May 2014. Oregon was the first U.S. state to legalize [physician-assisted suicide](/wiki/Physician-assisted_suicide "Physician-assisted suicide"), with the [Death with Dignity Act](/wiki/Oregon_Death_with_Dignity_Act "Oregon Death with Dignity Act") of 1994. Washington State was the second when I-1000 passed in 2008. [Colegio Cesar Chavez](/wiki/Colegio_Cesar_Chavez "Colegio Cesar Chavez"), the first fully accredited Hispanic college in the U.S., was founded in [Mount Angel, Oregon](/wiki/Mount_Angel,_Oregon "Mount Angel, Oregon"), in 1973. In 1986, [King County, Washington](/wiki/King_County,_Washington "King County, Washington"), which contains Seattle, voted to change its namesake from [William R. King](/wiki/William_R._King "William R. King") to [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.")[[115]](#cite_note-123)

These areas, especially around Puget Sound, have a long history of political radicalism. The radical labor organizers called [Wobblies](/wiki/Wobblies "Wobblies") were particularly strong there in the mines, [lumber camps](/wiki/Lumber_camp "Lumber camp") and shipyards. A number of [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism") communes sprang up there in the early 20th century (see Charles Pierce LeWarne's *Utopias on Puget Sound, 1885–1915* for an overview of this movement). There are also pro gun socialist organizations such as [Puget Sound John Brown Gun Club](/wiki/Puget_Sound_John_Brown_Gun_Club "Puget Sound John Brown Gun Club"). Seattle is one of a handful of major cities in North America in which the populace engaged in a [general strike](/wiki/Seattle_General_Strike "Seattle General Strike") (in 1919), and was the first major American city to elect a woman mayor, [Bertha Knight Landes](/wiki/Bertha_Knight_Landes "Bertha Knight Landes") (in 1926).[[116]](#cite_note-124) [Socialist](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") beliefs were once widespread (thanks in large part to the area's large numbers of Scandinavian immigrants), and the region has had a number of Socialist elected officials. So great was its influence that the U.S. Postmaster General, James Farley, jokingly toasted the "forty-seven states of the Union, and the Soviet of Washington", at a gala dinner in 1936 (although Farley denied ever saying it).[[117]](#cite_note-125)

Due to the Pacific Northwest being a generally liberal region, it also has a long history of feminism and people with feminist ideologies. The journey on the Oregon Trail may have been the part of the cause of feminism in the region, many women on the trail had to break gender-normative roles.[[118]](#cite_note-:0-126) Women occasionally were allowed the chance to try new things like cracking the whip for the wagon, given these opportunities women began to question their roles in society.[[118]](#cite_note-:0-126) Early days in the west, no forms of government had been established and this may have been part of the cause of feminist ideologies, new laws were formed to fit the regions needs and women were granted rights to land ownership in the West much earlier than in the East because of high death rates of men in the region.[[119]](#cite_note-127) While this may be coincidental, this granted women power. Women's suffrage movements were prominent in the Pacific Northwest; Susan B. Anthony did a tour through the region attempting to spread her ideas and made stops in Portland, the Willamette Valley, Columbia River, and Victoria.[[120]](#cite_note-128) Not only were women's suffrage movements prominent in the Pacific Northwest, but there was also a fight for women to keep their jobs after men returned from war in World War I.[[121]](#cite_note-129) A group titled the Washington State Women's Council (founded in 1963) fought for women's policies, this group worked towards the states' equal rights amendment, and fought for women's property rights in marriage during the 1972 legislative session.[[122]](#cite_note-130)

The region also has a long history of starting cooperative and communal businesses and organizations, including [Group Health](/wiki/Group_Health "Group Health"),[[123]](#cite_note-131) [REI](/wiki/REI "REI"), [MEC](/wiki/Mountain_Equipment_Co-op "Mountain Equipment Co-op"), [Puget Consumers Co-op](/wiki/Puget_Consumers_Co-op "Puget Consumers Co-op"), and numerous granges and mutual aid societies. It also has a long history of publicly owned power and utilities, with many of the region's cities owning their own [public utilities](/wiki/Public_utility "Public utility"). In British Columbia, [credit unions](/wiki/Credit_union "Credit union") are common and popular cooperatively owned financial institutions.

East of the Cascades, in Eastern Washington and Eastern Oregon, the population is much more conservative. The eastern portions of Washington and especially Oregon, due to their low populations, do not generally have enough voting power to be competitive at the state level, and thus the governorships and U.S. Senate seats of both Oregon and Washington are usually held by the [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"). Conservatism in the eastern part of the Pacific Northwest tends to be distrustful of federal government interference in the market.

[Aluminum smelting](/wiki/Aluminum_smelting "Aluminum smelting") was once an important part of the region's economy due to the abundance of cheap hydroelectric power. [Hydroelectric power](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") generated by the [hydroelectric dams on the Columbia River](/wiki/Hydroelectric_dams_on_the_Columbia_River "Hydroelectric dams on the Columbia River") powered at least ten aluminum smelters during the mid-20th century. By the end of World War II these smelters were producing over a third of the United States' aluminum. Production rose during the 1950s and 1960s, then declined. By the first decade of the 21st century the aluminum industry in the Pacific Northwest was essentially defunct.[[124]](#cite_note-132) The [Alcan](/wiki/Alcan "Alcan") smelter at [Kitimat](/wiki/Kitimat,_British_Columbia "Kitimat, British Columbia") continues in operation and is fed by the diversion of the [Nechako River](/wiki/Nechako_River "Nechako River") (a tributary of the Fraser) to a powerhouse on the coast at [Kemano](/wiki/Kemano "Kemano"), near Kitimat.

The region as a whole, but especially several specific areas, are concentrated high-tech areas: Seattle eastern suburbs, the Portland [Silicon Forest](/wiki/Silicon_Forest "Silicon Forest") area, and Vancouver, British Columbia. These areas are also leading "[creative class](/wiki/Creative_class "Creative class")" economic drivers, feeding thriving cultural sectors, and include many knowledge workers and numerous international advertising, media, and design firms.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Cascadia_Flag_%287134901861%29.jpg/250px-Cascadia_Flag_%287134901861%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cascadia_Flag_(7134901861).jpg)

A man in [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon") with [Cascadian flag](/wiki/Doug_flag "Doug flag") on [International Workers' Day](/wiki/May_Day "May Day"), 2012

Although the dominant culture in the Pacific Northwest today is [Anglo-American](/wiki/Anglo-Americans "Anglo-Americans"), [Anglo-Canadian](/wiki/English_Canadian "English Canadian"), and [Scandinavian American](/wiki/Scandinavian_Americans "Scandinavian Americans"), there is significant [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_people "Mexican people") and [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people") influence. 23% of Vancouver, British Columbia, is Chinese, and 50% of residents of the City of Vancouver do not speak English as their first language.[[125]](#cite_note-133) Parts of Oregon and Washington are [bilingual](/wiki/Bilingual "Bilingual") in both English and Spanish, and Native American culture is strong throughout the Pacific Northwest.

### Counterculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Counterculture")]

The Pacific Northwest's liberal political leanings and remote location have given the region a reputation for counterculture.[[126]](#cite_note-134) This reputation includes the region's major cities, particularly Portland,[[127]](#cite_note-135) but also extends to smaller urban areas such as [Olympia, Washington](/wiki/Olympia,_Washington "Olympia, Washington") and [Eugene, Oregon](/wiki/Eugene,_Oregon "Eugene, Oregon").[[128]](#cite_note-136)[[129]](#cite_note-137) This reputation may stem from the [hippie movement](/wiki/Hippie_movement "Hippie movement"), which began in the Pacific Northwest and California.

### Drug use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Drug use")]

[Cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") use is relatively popular, especially around Vancouver, Victoria, Bellingham, Seattle, Olympia, Portland, and Eugene. Several of these jurisdictions have made arrests for cannabis a low enforcement priority. Medical marijuana is legal in British Columbia,[[130]](#cite_note-138) Washington,[[131]](#cite_note-139) and Oregon,[[132]](#cite_note-140) as well as in Alaska, which has legalised cannabis and has many licensed dispensaries,[[133]](#cite_note-141) and in Yukon, although fewer than 50 of the territory's residents are licensed to use medical marijuana, and no legal dispensaries operate within its borders.[[134]](#cite_note-142) As of December 6, 2012, possession of less than an ounce of marijuana for recreational use by persons over 21 years of age became legal in Washington state as a result of state ballot measure [Initiative 502](/wiki/Washington_Initiative_502 "Washington Initiative 502"), which was approved by the state's voters on November 6, 2012, by a ten-point margin. As of July 1, 2015, recreational marijuana use was legalized in Oregon as well.[[135]](#cite_note-143)[[136]](#cite_note-144)

### Environmentalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Environmentalism")]

[Environmentalism](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism") is prominent throughout the region, especially west of the Cascades. Environmentally conscious services such as [recycling](/wiki/Recycling "Recycling") and [public transportation](/wiki/Public_transportation "Public transportation") are widespread, most notably in the more populous areas. Politically, the Pacific Northwest is actively involved in environmental efforts. The international organization [Greenpeace](/wiki/Greenpeace "Greenpeace") was born in Vancouver in 1970 as part of a large public opposition movement in British Columbia to US nuclear weapons testing on [Amchitka Island](/wiki/Amchitka_Island "Amchitka Island") in the Aleutian Islands. Liberal and conservative Northwesterners, such as former U.S. Senator [Slade Gorton](/wiki/Slade_Gorton "Slade Gorton") (R-WA) and moderate Democrats like former Speaker of the House [Tom Foley](/wiki/Tom_Foley "Tom Foley") (D-WA), have been prominent in the development of conservative approaches to environmental protection. Seattle in particular is also home to a large number of publications and institutions concerned with the environment and sustainability, including both *[Worldchanging](/wiki/Worldchanging "Worldchanging")* and *[Grist.org](/wiki/Grist_(magazine) "Grist (magazine)")*, the U.S.'s two largest online green magazines. The Pacific Northwest is also noted for a large number of gardening clubs, with Victoria having an annual flower count in February.

The direct-intervention oceanic protection group known as the [Sea Shepherd Conservation Society](/wiki/Sea_Shepherd_Conservation_Society "Sea Shepherd Conservation Society") has its headquarters in [Friday Harbor](/wiki/Friday_Harbor,_Washington "Friday Harbor, Washington") on [San Juan Island](/wiki/San_Juan_Island "San Juan Island").[[137]](#cite_note-145)

In British Columbia, environmentalists fought to protect [Clayoquot Sound](/wiki/Clayoquot_Sound "Clayoquot Sound") in the 1980s and 1990s. More recently the province has agreed to environmental protections in the [Great Bear Rainforest](/wiki/Great_Bear_Rainforest "Great Bear Rainforest").

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [Music of the Pacific Northwest](/wiki/Music_of_the_Pacific_Northwest "Music of the Pacific Northwest")

The Pacific Northwest has a rich history in incubating and promoting musicians. It is best known for [indie music](/wiki/Indie_(music) "Indie (music)"), especially [grunge](/wiki/Grunge "Grunge"), [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock"), and [metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music"); the region also has [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") and [world music](/wiki/World_music "World music") traditions and has lately gained notice for its [hip hop scene](/wiki/Hip_hop_music_in_the_Pacific_Northwest "Hip hop music in the Pacific Northwest"). Many acts are associated with the record label [Sub Pop](/wiki/Sub_Pop "Sub Pop"), based in Seattle; independent labels [K Records](/wiki/K_Records "K Records") of Olympia and [Kill Rock Stars](/wiki/Kill_Rock_Stars "Kill Rock Stars") of Olympia and Portland are also prominent.

The Seattle-based radio station [KEXP](/wiki/KEXP "KEXP") has gained a national reputation for promoting [indie](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") and alternative music.[[138]](#cite_note-146) Among the Northwest's largest music festivals are the [Merritt Mountain Music Festival](/wiki/Merritt_Mountain_Music_Festival "Merritt Mountain Music Festival"), the [Vancouver Folk Music Festival](/wiki/Vancouver_Folk_Music_Festival "Vancouver Folk Music Festival"), the [Sasquatch! Music Festival](/wiki/Sasquatch!_Music_Festival "Sasquatch! Music Festival") in [George, Washington](/wiki/George,_Washington "George, Washington"), Seattle's [Bumbershoot](/wiki/Bumbershoot "Bumbershoot"), Boise's [Treefort Music Fest](/wiki/Treefort_Music_Fest "Treefort Music Fest"), and Portland's [MusicfestNW](/wiki/MusicfestNW "MusicfestNW"); smaller festivals across the region are held frequently. Portland's Waterfront Blues Festival is the largest blues-based festival west of the Mississippi River.

Among the most notable rock artists originating from the region are [Elliott Smith](/wiki/Elliott_Smith "Elliott Smith"), [Jimi Hendrix](/wiki/Jimi_Hendrix "Jimi Hendrix"), [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)"), [Modest Mouse](/wiki/Modest_Mouse "Modest Mouse"), [Pearl Jam](/wiki/Pearl_Jam "Pearl Jam"), [Soundgarden](/wiki/Soundgarden "Soundgarden"), [Alice in Chains](/wiki/Alice_in_Chains "Alice in Chains"), [Foo Fighters](/wiki/Foo_Fighters "Foo Fighters"), [Mother Mother](/wiki/Mother_Mother "Mother Mother"), [the Decemberists](/wiki/The_Decemberists "The Decemberists"), and [Sleater-Kinney](/wiki/Sleater-Kinney "Sleater-Kinney"). The history of Northwest rock, however, finds its roots in the mid-1950s and 1960s with such bands as [the Sonics](/wiki/The_Sonics "The Sonics"), [the Ventures](/wiki/The_Ventures "The Ventures"), [the Kingsmen](/wiki/The_Kingsmen "The Kingsmen"), and [Paul Revere and the Raiders](/wiki/Paul_Revere_%26_the_Raiders "Paul Revere & the Raiders").[[139]](#cite_note-147) Pop and hip-hop musicians including [Quincy Jones](/wiki/Quincy_Jones "Quincy Jones"), [Macklemore](/wiki/Macklemore "Macklemore"), and [Amine](/wiki/Amine "Amine") also hail from the region.[[140]](#cite_note-148)

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Pacific Northwest cuisine](/wiki/Pacific_Northwest_cuisine "Pacific Northwest cuisine")

Foods typical of the Pacific Northwest include [wild salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon"), [halibut](/wiki/Halibut "Halibut"), [shellfish](/wiki/Shellfish "Shellfish"), [huckleberries](/wiki/Huckleberry "Huckleberry"), [marionberries](/wiki/Marionberry "Marionberry"), a wide variety of [Asian cuisines](/wiki/Asian_cuisine "Asian cuisine"), and locally produced fruits, vegetables, and cheeses.[[141]](#cite_note-jackson-149)[[142]](#cite_note-150)

[Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine"), [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_cuisine "Japanese cuisine"), [Korean](/wiki/Korean_cuisine "Korean cuisine"), [Indian](/wiki/Indian_cuisine "Indian cuisine"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine"), Mexican, and [Greek cuisines](/wiki/Greek_cuisine "Greek cuisine") are prevalent throughout the Northwest, and reflect the historically strong presence of those communities in the restaurant industry there. [Teriyaki](/wiki/Teriyaki "Teriyaki") restaurants are particularly common in the Seattle area.[[143]](#cite_note-edge-151)[[144]](#cite_note-kauffman-152) Eateries featuring [West Asian](/wiki/West_Asian_cuisine "West Asian cuisine") (predominantly [Persian](/wiki/Persian_cuisine "Persian cuisine")), [East Asian fusion](/wiki/Asian_cuisine "Asian cuisine"), and [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asian_cuisine "South Asian cuisine") (predominantly [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_cuisine "Punjabi cuisine")) cuisines are common throughout in Greater Vancouver, as are ethnic specialty restaurants of all kinds. Ethnic staples ranging from frozen [pierogi](/wiki/Pierogi "Pierogi") or [samosas](/wiki/Samosas "Samosas") to frozen [spring rolls](/wiki/Spring_rolls "Spring rolls") or [dim sum](/wiki/Dim_sum "Dim sum") are common in most supermarkets in these communities.

Locally made craft beers, ciders, and premium wines from various wine-growing areas within the region are popular with drinkers and diners. Northern latitude and coastal breezes create a climate that attracts international recognition for its mostly family-owned and operated vineyards and wineries.

Portland is a major microbrewery center in America,[[145]](#cite_note-153) and is home to [numerous breweries](/wiki/List_of_Oregon_breweries "List of Oregon breweries").

### Separatism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Separatism")]

There have been proposals for certain parts of the Pacific Northwest becoming its own country because of the shared [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion") and culture,[[146]](#cite_note-154)[[147]](#cite_note-155) the most well-known being [Cascadia](/wiki/Cascadia_(independence_movement) "Cascadia (independence movement)"). However, the region is strongly divided by the international border, and this division grew more rather than less powerful over the 20th century.[[148]](#cite_note-abbott-156) Carl Abbott argues that, given the twin factors of limited economic integration vis-a-vis NAFTA, and cultural similarities, he views the major cities as "going their separate ways" as east–west gateways of commerce, competing with each other, rather than forming north–south connectors of a tentative "mega-region".[[148]](#cite_note-abbott-156)

Vancouver is home to a 4-team league for [Australian football](/wiki/Australian_football "Australian football"), the British Columbia Australian Football League, one of several [Canadian Australian football leagues](/wiki/Australian_rules_football_in_Canada "Australian rules football in Canada").

Hockey is the most popular spectator sport in British Columbia, with the [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks") of the [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL") being the most popular professional team, although the [Vancouver Giants](/wiki/Vancouver_Giants "Vancouver Giants") of the [Western Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League "Western Hockey League") also have a very strong following. The [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League")'s BC Lions are considered Vancouver's second most popular team, although [major league soccer](/wiki/Major_league_soccer "Major league soccer")'s Vancouver Whitecaps FC have been rising in popularity in recent years. Hockey is slowly gaining popularity south of the border too, with the [Everett Silvertips](/wiki/Everett_Silvertips "Everett Silvertips"), [Portland Winterhawks](/wiki/Portland_Winterhawks "Portland Winterhawks"), and [Seattle Thunderbirds](/wiki/Seattle_Thunderbirds "Seattle Thunderbirds") of the [Western Hockey League](/wiki/Western_Hockey_League "Western Hockey League") and the [Seattle Kraken](/wiki/Seattle_Kraken "Seattle Kraken") of the [NHL](/wiki/NHL "NHL").

Followers of the Portland Trail Blazers basketball team refer to themselves as the *Sixth Man* and *Blazermania* refers to the extraordinary dedication fans have shown the team. In Seattle, many fans are still upset over the move of the [Seattle SuperSonics](/wiki/Seattle_SuperSonics "Seattle SuperSonics") while supporters of the Seattle Seahawks football team are known officially as the 12th Man.[[150]](#cite_note-158) And the [supporter groups](/wiki/Supporter_groups "Supporter groups"), (namely the [Emerald City Supporters](/wiki/Emerald_City_Supporters "Emerald City Supporters"), [Timbers Army](/wiki/Timbers_Army "Timbers Army"), and [Vancouver Southsiders](/wiki/Vancouver_Southsiders "Vancouver Southsiders")) of the three MLS teams of the region are renowned for their passion and dedication to their teams.

The only major track for motorsports in the Pacific Northwest region is [Portland International Raceway](/wiki/Portland_International_Raceway "Portland International Raceway"). PIR currently hosts the NTT [IndyCar Series](/wiki/IndyCar_Series "IndyCar Series") and its [Road to Indy](/wiki/Road_to_Indy "Road to Indy") ladder series', as well as the [NASCAR Xfinity Series](/wiki/NASCAR_Xfinity_Series "NASCAR Xfinity Series"); the number two stock car series in the country. [Evergreen Speedway](/wiki/Evergreen_Speedway "Evergreen Speedway"), north and east of Seattle, is the largest short track west of the Mississippi River and has hosted many of the marquee drivers of NASCAR. With three oval tracks, a figure eight track and various road course variants, Evergreen Speedway operates year-round events. Evergreen Speedway hosts the NASCAR Whelen All American Series, the ARCA Menards Series West, National Figure Eight Events, USAC, SCCA, plus Touring Groups and Formula Drift. The Northwest's most successful racers on a national platform include [1983 Indianapolis 500](/wiki/1983_Indianapolis_500 "1983 Indianapolis 500") winner [Tom Sneva](/wiki/Tom_Sneva "Tom Sneva") (Spokane), [Greg Moore](/wiki/Greg_Moore_(racing_driver) "Greg Moore (racing driver)") (Maple Ridge, BC), [Greg Biffle](/wiki/Greg_Biffle "Greg Biffle"), (Vancouver, WA), [Kasey Kahne](/wiki/Kasey_Kahne "Kasey Kahne") (Enumclaw, WA), and [1990 Daytona 500](/wiki/1990_Daytona_500 "1990 Daytona 500") winner [Derrike Cope](/wiki/Derrike_Cope "Derrike Cope") (Spanaway, WA). Also, [Mike Bliss](/wiki/Mike_Bliss "Mike Bliss") (Milwaukee, OR), [Chuck Bown](/wiki/Chuck_Bown "Chuck Bown") (Portland), and [Chad Little](/wiki/Chad_Little "Chad Little") (Spokane) have won NASCAR-sanctioned championships, and had lengthy careers in NASCAR's 3 national series.

In Idaho, Washington and Oregon, many residents passionately follow [college athletics](/wiki/College_athletics "College athletics"). In Washington, the major [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") college athletic programs are the [University of Washington Huskies](/wiki/University_of_Washington_Huskies "University of Washington Huskies"). In Oregon, the major programs are the [University of Oregon Ducks](/wiki/University_of_Oregon_Ducks "University of Oregon Ducks") and the [Oregon State Beavers](/wiki/Oregon_State_Beavers "Oregon State Beavers"). All four of these programs are members of the [Pac-12 Conference](/wiki/Pac-12_Conference "Pac-12 Conference") and compete with each other in a variety of sports. These universities are all considered rivals of one another, particularly in [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football"). The most significant of these rivalries are the [Oregon–Washington football rivalry](/wiki/Oregon%E2%80%93Washington_football_rivalry "Oregon–Washington football rivalry") game, the Washington-Washington State game known as the [Apple Cup](/wiki/Apple_Cup "Apple Cup") due to Washington's notoriety for apple production and the [Oregon–Oregon State football rivalry](/wiki/Oregon%E2%80%93Oregon_State_football_rivalry "Oregon–Oregon State football rivalry"). As in professional sports, college fans in the Pacific Northwest are known for being particularly passionate about their teams. Both [Husky Stadium](/wiki/Husky_Stadium "Husky Stadium") (where the Washington Huskies play football) and [Autzen Stadium](/wiki/Autzen_Stadium "Autzen Stadium") (where the Oregon Ducks play football) have gained reputations for deafening noise, despite not being the largest of college football venues. Husky Stadium currently holds the record for the loudest crowd noise in NCAA history at 130 [decibels](/wiki/Decibels "Decibels"),[[151]](#cite_note-159) while Autzen Stadium currently holds the record for the 4th at 127 decibels.[[152]](#cite_note-160) In Idaho, the major [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") college athletic programs are the [Boise State Broncos](/wiki/Boise_State_Broncos "Boise State Broncos"), the [Idaho Vandals](/wiki/Idaho_Vandals "Idaho Vandals") and the [Idaho State Bengals](/wiki/Idaho_State_Bengals "Idaho State Bengals"), the latter two being members of the [Football Championship Subdivision](/wiki/Football_Championship_Subdivision "Football Championship Subdivision") in the [Big Sky Conference](/wiki/Big_Sky_Conference "Big Sky Conference") while Boise State competes in the [Mountain West Conference](/wiki/Mountain_West_Conference "Mountain West Conference") of the [Football Bowl Subdivision](/wiki/Football_Bowl_Subdivision "Football Bowl Subdivision"). Boise State and Idaho enjoyed a healthy rivalry from the 1970s through the late 2000s with each team having significant win streaks in the series over the other, Boise State had the most recent streak with 12 consecutive wins starting in 1999 which was preceded by Idaho's most recent win streak of 12 consecutive wins from 1982 to 1993. In 2018, Idaho rekindled an old rivalry with Idaho State that had been dormant since 1996 when Idaho moved up to FBS. Idaho currently has the lead in the [Battle of the Domes](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Domes "Battle of the Domes") series 29–13.

### Video games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Video games")]

Seattle is considered by *Digital Trends* magazine to be the top gaming city in America, a possible indicator of markedly higher rates of video game usage throughout the Pacific Northwest in general.[[153]](#cite_note-vg-161) A number of major companies are headquartered in the Seattle metropolitan area, including [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), [Valve](/wiki/Valve_Corporation "Valve Corporation"), [Bungie](/wiki/Bungie "Bungie"), [Nintendo of America](/wiki/Nintendo_of_America "Nintendo of America") (a wholly owned subsidiary of [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo")), and [Sony Computer Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Computer_Entertainment "Sony Computer Entertainment")'s subsidiary [Sucker Punch Productions](/wiki/Sucker_Punch_Productions "Sucker Punch Productions"). Microsoft and Nintendo of America also have Canadian branches headquartered in Vancouver—Microsoft Canada and Nintendo of Canada—respectively, while [EA Vancouver](/wiki/EA_Vancouver "EA Vancouver") (a subsidiary division of [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts")) is in the same city.

### Self-determination movements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Self-determination movements")]

Among the fiercely independent and frontier nature of the former Oregon Country and now western part of the United States, is the desire of some Pacific Northwesterners to improve upon their form of democracy by further subdividing the region into [socio-political](/wiki/Socio-political "Socio-political") or [bioregion](/wiki/Bioregion "Bioregion") defined [nation states](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state"). Some desires are [transnational](/wiki/Transnationality "Transnationality") and autonomous of the United States while others are in the hope of gaining additional representational control in particular regions of the Northwest. Among these fluidly changing geographical boundaries and areas sought by a segment of the population of the Northwest are the following Pacific Northwest proposed states and [separatist movements](/wiki/Separatist_movement "Separatist movement"):

* [Cascadia](/wiki/Cascadia_(independence_movement) "Cascadia (independence movement)")
* [Jefferson](/wiki/Jefferson_(proposed_Pacific_state) "Jefferson (proposed Pacific state)")
* [Lincoln](/wiki/Lincoln_(proposed_Northwestern_state) "Lincoln (proposed Northwestern state)")
* [Northwest Territory](/wiki/Northwest_Territorial_Imperative "Northwest Territorial Imperative")

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Further information: [Amtrak Cascades](/wiki/Amtrak_Cascades "Amtrak Cascades"), [Coast Starlight](/wiki/Coast_Starlight "Coast Starlight"), [Interstate 5](/wiki/Interstate_5 "Interstate 5"), [Interstate 90](/wiki/Interstate_90 "Interstate 90"), [Transportation in Seattle](/wiki/Transportation_in_Seattle "Transportation in Seattle"), [Transportation in Portland](/wiki/Transportation_in_Portland "Transportation in Portland"), and [Transportation in Vancouver](/wiki/Transportation_in_Vancouver "Transportation in Vancouver")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/29th_Avenue_platform_level_%2820190626_123343%29.jpg/250px-29th_Avenue_platform_level_%2820190626_123343%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:29th_Avenue_platform_level_(20190626_123343).jpg)

Public transportation is used in the Pacific Northwest region. Vancouver's [SkyTrain](/wiki/SkyTrain_(Vancouver) "SkyTrain (Vancouver)") rapid transit system achieves daily ridership of over 500,000 passengers per day on weekdays and the overall transit ridership levels in the [Metro Vancouver](/wiki/Metro_Vancouver "Metro Vancouver") area rank third in North America per capita.[[154]](#cite_note-162)

A 2007 statistical analysis ranked the 50 Greenest Cities in the United States, placing Portland, Oregon first, Eugene, Oregon, fifth, and Seattle, Washington, eighth.[[155]](#cite_note-163) The region as a whole is also known for its [bicycle culture](/wiki/Bicycle_culture "Bicycle culture") as an alternative form of transportation; Portland is considered by Forbes Traveler to be the second most [bicycle-friendly](/wiki/Bicycle-friendly "Bicycle-friendly") city in the world.[[156]](#cite_note-MSNBC-164) Portland is also the hub of American bicycle manufacturing; as a whole it generated over $68 million in revenue in 2007.[[156]](#cite_note-MSNBC-164)

### Transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pacific_Northwest&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Transit")]

Seattle, Washington has also garnered a reputation for its contributions to public transportation with the [Puget Sound Transit system](/wiki/Sound_Transit "Sound Transit"), including an underground light rail system and a 38.9% worker commute rate as of 2011.[[157]](#cite_note-165) Mass transit in Portland Metropolitan area is provided by [TriMet](/wiki/TriMet "TriMet") and in Vancouver by [TransLink (British Columbia)](/wiki/TransLink_(British_Columbia) "TransLink (British Columbia)").

Some tribal governments offer [free bus service](/wiki/Free_public_transport "Free public transport") on their respective reservations, including on the [Muckleshoot](/wiki/Muckleshoot_Indian_Reservation "Muckleshoot Indian Reservation"),[[158]](#cite_note-166) [Spokane](/wiki/Spokane_Indian_Reservation "Spokane Indian Reservation"),[[159]](#cite_note-167) [Umatilla](/wiki/Umatilla_Indian_Reservation "Umatilla Indian Reservation")[[160]](#cite_note-168) and [Yakama Indian Reservations](/wiki/Yakama_Indian_Reservation "Yakama Indian Reservation").[[161]](#cite_note-169)
